name: Area
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a distinct inktrap variant across all widths, spanning compressed through extended cuts in the manner of a workhorse display and text system.
summary: A 176-style grotesque system from Blaze Type spanning four widths, eleven weights and a full inktrap variant family, all available as variable fonts.
description: Eight sub-families run from Compressed to Extended, each available in standard and inktrap versions, with eleven weights from Hairline to Extrablack and matching italics throughout. The inktrap cuts introduce deliberate ink-trap notches at stroke junctions, giving a technical quality that reads well at both display and text sizes. The variable font format lets designers move fluidly across the weight and width axes within each subfamily.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Area as a collection of eight families totalling 176 styles, offering Compressed, Condensed, Normal and Extended widths each paired with an Inktrap counterpart, all available as variable fonts spanning Hairline to Extrablack with italics throughout.

name: Fusion
classification_notes: The family splits into two stylistic branches, Neue and Grotesk, both sans-serif with no serifs or slab elements, spanning standard and expanded widths in the manner of a contemporary grotesque system.
summary: A 72-style grotesque system from Blaze Type split across two stylistic branches, Neue and Grotesk, each available in standard and expanded widths with nine weights and matching italics.
description: The family pairs a Neue branch with a Grotesk branch, giving designers two distinct but related voices within a single coherent system. Both branches run across standard and expanded width variants, with nine weights from Thin to Black and corresponding italics in each. Variable font support is included, making the full range of 72 styles accessible across a single file.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Fusion as a collection of four families spanning 72 styles and a variable font, comprising Neue, Grotesk, Neue Expanded and Grotesk Expanded subfamilies.

name: Kurs
classification_notes: Kurs is explicitly described as a display serif, with stark thick-thin contrasts, spiky serifs and expressive terminals that are characteristic of high-contrast display work rather than text use.
summary: A high-contrast display serif by Sandro Bonomo, built around stark thick-thin transitions and spiky serifs drawn from a conceptual rereading of the Renaissance.
description: Designed by Sandro Bonomo and released in 2024, the family spans six weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, each with an italic, and is also available as a variable font. The design draws on the instability of transitional periods, translating that tension into sharp serifs, flat curves and opposing stroke weights. Available in TTF, WOFF, WOFF2 and variable formats, it covers Latin European languages.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Kurs as a display serif inspired by the concept of the 'New Renaissance', which rethinks that historical period as an anxious transition between the Medieval and Modernity. Shifting paradigms and societal insecurity are translated into shapes through stark contrasts, spiky serifs and opposing soft and flat curves. The name Kurs is the German word for 'course', chosen for its thematic fit and its phonetic resonance with words like curse, cursive and kursiv.

name: Surt
classification_notes: Surt presents as a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a structured width system across Normal, Expanded and Extended families, consistent with neo-grotesque or geometric grotesque conventions.
summary: A 60-style grotesque sans from Blaze Type spanning three width families and ten weights, with full italic coverage and variable font support.
description: Surt is structured around three distinct width families: Normal, Expanded and Extended, each running from Thin to Black with matching italics. The 60-style collection is available as a variable font, giving fine-grained control over weight across the width axis. The range suits editorial, identity and display work where consistent width variation is needed across a single type system.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Surt as a collection of three families spanning Normal, Expanded and Extended widths, offered in 60 styles with variable font support.

name: Yowza
classification_notes: The family's optical size range, soft and standard variants, and warm proportions point to a humanist sans structure; the 'Soft' subfamilies with rounded terminals reinforce that classification over geometric or grotesque.
summary: An 84-style humanist sans from Blaze Type spanning seven subfamilies across display, standard and text optical sizes, with sharp and softened variants throughout.
description: The collection divides into two broad strands: standard cuts with conventional terminals and Soft cuts with rounded endings, each available in Display, Standard and Text sizes for optical appropriateness at different scales. A Mix subfamily sits alongside these, and all seven families share the same six weights from Regular to Black with matching italics. The range is built for systems-level deployment where a single typeface family must cover headings, body copy and interface text without stylistic discontinuity.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Yowza as a collection of seven families totalling 84 styles, structured around optical size variants and a soft/standard distinction, giving designers a single cohesive system capable of covering display through text settings.

name: Medieval
classification_notes: Blaze Type describe it as a transitional typeface, derived from Ehmcke's 1922 Mediaeval; its 45° serif geometry, ink traps and translation contrast align with transitional serif conventions while drawing on Renaissance and early 20th-century type history.
summary: A reinterpretation of Fritz Helmuth Ehmcke's 1922 Mediaeval, introducing 45° serif geometry and ink traps to sharpen the original's tall ascenders and translation contrast into something more controlled.
description: Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and Pauline Fourest and released in 2026, this single-weight serif draws on Stempel's 1922 Mediaeval as its model while trading period looseness for careful geometric precision. The 45° serif segments and ink traps impose discipline without erasing the historical warmth; overall contrast is kept restrained to maintain readability at text sizes. Tall ascenders and architectural rhythm make it well-suited to logotypes, editorial headlines and cultural institution work, where its historical resonance carries weight.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Medieval as a free interpretation of Ehmcke's Stempel Mediaeval (1922), shaped by the challenge of staying faithful to the original's distinctive qualities — versatile serifs, tall ascenders and pronounced translation contrast — while achieving something cleaner and more controlled. The approach centres on 45° serif segments and ink traps for quiet geometric precision. Certain characters were deliberately softened, with the 'o' taking on expansion contrast and overall contrast held back for sustained readability. The result, they say, is a transitional typeface with deep historical sensibility: structurally disciplined but warm enough for body text, and most at home in display work such as logotypes, editorial headlines and book covers.

name: Amsterdam Serif
classification_notes: A revival of an early 20th-century wood typeface with calligraphic terminations, stabilised for contemporary use across text and display settings.
summary: A revival of a wood typeface found in a Brussels printing workshop, reinterpreted with calligraphic detail across nine weights and matching italics.
description: Based on wooden letters discovered in the type case of Unitype, a Brussels printing workshop, the design preserves the original's distinctive terminations while stabilising inherited shapes for use at both text and display sizes. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, are supplied as variable fonts alongside static cuts. The family is intended for editorial systems, signage and branding where a historical flavour is needed without period eccentricity.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Amsterdam Serif as a generous reinterpretation of an early 20th-century wood typeface, discovered in a Brussels printing workshop called Unitype. They cite its distinctive letterform terminations as the defining characteristic, and position it as a workhorse family that bridges historical revival and contemporary refinement for use in editorial design, signage systems and global visual identities.

name: Cuptcha
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans-serif with no visible serif bracketing or humanist influence, offered across a broad width axis from Compressed to Wide suggesting a neutral, functional grotesque construction.
summary: A large-scale grotesque collection from Blaze Type spanning six widths and nine weights, available as a variable font across 108 styles.
description: Six width families run from Compressed to Wide, each carrying nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, totalling 108 styles. The variable font format allows continuous interpolation across both the weight and width axes. The breadth of the system makes it suited to identity and editorial work where a single type family must cover many different spatial conditions.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Cuptcha as a comprehensive collection of six families spanning compressed through wide widths, with each family offering a full range of weights and italics, all available as a variable font totalling 108 styles.

name: Lyst
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a condensed serif drawing on the editorial serif tradition of the late 20th century, with narrow proportions and vertical tension; the initial conceptual spark was mechanically condensed Times New Roman.
summary: A condensed serif with narrow proportions and a disciplined vertical rhythm, drawing loosely on the editorial atmosphere of late 20th-century print.
description: Built from the observation of mechanically condensed Times New Roman in everyday print, Lyst treats that compression not as a defect but as a formal starting point. The 27 styles span nine weights with Roman, true Italic and Slanted cuts, the latter set at a precise 10° angle to mirror the conceptual logic of mechanical inclination. The result sits somewhere between corporate clarity and magazine sophistication without committing firmly to either.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Lyst as a condensed serif shaped by the visual culture of the late 20th century, capturing an editorial atmosphere where corporate clarity met magazine sophistication. The starting point was mechanically condensed Times New Roman in everyday print, treated not as a flaw but as a conceptual premise for exploring narrow proportions and disciplined rhythm. Slanted cuts at 10° extend that logic into movement, complementing the true Italics across a 27-style family available as a variable font.

name: Sizek Pro
classification_notes: A large-scale grotesque sans-serif family spanning seven width variants from Compressed to Normal, each with a full weight range and standard, italic and super italic styles. The inclusion of a 'Super Italic' cut and compressed extremes places it in the expressive grotesque tradition.
summary: A 210-style grotesque spanning seven widths from Compressed to Normal, each carrying ten weights and three slope variants including a super italic.
description: Sizek Pro is a large grotesque family from Blaze Type built around seven width grades, running from Compressed through to Normal, with each width offering ten weights from Thin to ExtraBold. Every weight ships in upright, italic and a steeper 'Super Italic' cut, bringing the total to 210 styles. The family is also available as a variable font, making it well suited to editorial and branding work that demands tight width control.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Sizek Pro as a collection of seven families — Compressed, Ultra Condensed, Extra Condensed, Condensed, Semi Condensed, Narrow and Normal — totalling 210 styles and available as a variable font.

name: Rules JP
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a contemporary neo-grotesque rooted in Swiss Modern type tradition, with near-zero contrast in counter-forms and squared terminals consistent with that genre.
summary: A neo-grotesque extended from the Apoc family to cover Latin, Hiragana and Katakana, with support for both horizontal and vertical Japanese typesetting.
description: Rules JP adds Japanese kana to the Rules/Apoc family, covering Hiragana, Katakana and extended Latin across nine weights in a variable file. The design follows Swiss Modern grotesque conventions: near-zero counter contrast, closed terminals and squared stroke endings that give text layouts an even optical grey. Both horizontal and vertical writing modes are supported, making it viable for body text in print, web and app contexts as well as headlines and signage.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Rules JP as the Latin and Japanese (kana) edition of the Apoc Font Family, supporting horizontal and vertical writing systems. They position Rules as a contemporary take on the neo-grotesque era with an edge, rooted in Swiss Modern type and built with modern capitals, near-zero counter contrast, closed-edge terminals and squared endings. It was designed with body text in print, web and app use in mind, while also performing well at display sizes and in signage systems.

name: Apoc JP
classification_notes: A sharp, high-contrast expressive display family designed explicitly for bold headline use, with a dramatic angular character set spanning Latin, Hiragana and Katakana.
summary: A Latin and Japanese Kana extension of the Apoc family, adding Hiragana and Katakana support with both horizontal and vertical writing modes across six variable weights.
description: Originally released in 2018 and updated through 2022, the Apoc family is now extended by Caio Kondo's Kana drawings to cover Hiragana and Katakana alongside the existing Latin, Cyrillic and Greek. The sharp, angular character set runs from Hairline to Dark as a variable font, and the vertical writing support makes it usable in Japanese layout conventions. Suited to display work where a consistent aggressive register is wanted across scripts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Apoc JP as the Latin and Japanese Kana version of the Apoc font family, supporting both horizontal and vertical writing systems. They characterise Apoc as embodying a battle between light and dark, with a sharp and expressive character set designed for bold standout display use including website headlines, art exhibition posters and album covers. The Kana extension was designed by Caio Kondo.

name: Area JP
classification_notes: Area JP is described explicitly as a variable geometric font family with neogrotesque characteristics; the modular geometric construction with a quiet, neutral tone places it firmly in the geometric-neogrotesque tradition.
summary: A variable geometric sans covering Latin and Japanese kana scripts, designed to support both horizontal and vertical writing systems across eleven weights.
description: Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and Caio Kondo, Area JP extends the Area font family with full Hiragana and Katakana support alongside extended Latin and Greek. The design begins from a modular geometric structure before settling into a quieter neogrotesque register, yielding even colour across diverse scripts. A variable font file spans Hairline to Extrablack, making it well-suited to multilingual publishing, branding and screen work where Japanese and Latin sit together.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Area JP as the Latin and Japanese kana extension of the Area Font Family, supporting both horizontal and vertical writing systems. Area itself is a variable geometric font family drawing inspiration from architecture, built on a stable modular geometric foundation that gradually opens into a quieter neogrotesque form, offering smooth grey and a solid base for reading experiences. Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and Caio Kondo and released in 2025.

name: Apoc
classification_notes: A deliberately expressive, sharp-edged display family with pointed terminals and high contrast detailing; designed primarily for headlines and poster use rather than extended text.
summary: A sharp, high-contrast display family from Blaze Type, released in 2018 and updated twice since, with two subfamilies across 24 styles including a variable file.
description: Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio with Cyrillic extensions by Ilya Ruderman and Yury Ostromentsky, the family splits into Normal and Revelations subfamilies, each running six weights from Hairline to Dark with matching italics. The pointed terminals and expressive stroke contrast make it well suited to exhibition posters, album artwork and display headlines where strong typographic presence is required. Coverage extends to Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, and the family ships as a variable font alongside individual static files.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Apoc as embodying the battle between Light and Dark, with a sharp character set and alternates first released in 2018 and updated in 2020 and 2022. They position it as the most popular typeface of standout contemporary independent design, calling it elegant, sharp and expressive, and suited to website headlines, exhibition posters and album covers.

name: Dean Soft
classification_notes: A soft-edged sans-serif with rounded terminals suggesting a humanist influence; the breadth of width variants from Compressed to Wide places it in the humanist sans tradition with display range.
summary: A 100-style variable sans-serif spanning five widths from Compressed to Wide, each in ten weights with matching italics.
description: Available as variable fonts across five named width families, Dean Soft covers an unusually broad typographic range in a single release. Each width runs from Thin to Black with a full set of italics, giving 20 styles per family and 100 in total. The rounded, soft character suits editorial and branding contexts where a geometric feel is wanted without sharp austerity.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Dean Soft as a 100-style collection organised into five families spanning Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Wide widths, each available as a variable font across ten weights with italics throughout.

name: Dean Soft Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif family with rounded, softened serifs across five width variants and ten weights, placing it firmly in the soft slab category rather than a hard geometric or Egyptian slab tradition.
summary: A large-scale soft slab serif available as a variable font across five widths and ten weights, each with matching italics, totalling 100 styles.
description: Spanning Compressed to Wide and Thin to Black, the family gives designers a broad tonal and spatial range within a single slab typeface. The softened serifs temper the robustness typical of Egyptian slabs, lending the family a more approachable character suited to both text and display use. Variable font versions are provided for each width subfamily, covering weight and italic axes.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Dean Soft Slab as a collection of five families — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Wide — each running through ten weights from Thin to Black with corresponding italics, totalling 100 styles and offered as variable fonts.

name: Offset
classification_notes: Offset is a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with an inktrap subfamily, indicating construction rooted in optical compensation traditions typical of refined grotesque type design.
summary: A grotesque sans in two subfamilies — Normal and Inktrap — spanning nine weights with italics and full variable font support.
description: The family runs across 36 styles divided between a standard Normal cut and an Inktrap variant, the latter incorporating ink-trap notches at stroke junctions for optical refinement at smaller sizes or in print contexts. Both subfamilies share the same nine-weight range from Thin to Heavy, each with matching italics. Available as variable fonts, the range suits editorial, branding and screen work where weight range and typographic detail matter.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Offset as a two-family collection — Normal and Inktrap — comprising 36 styles and offered as variable fonts, spanning Thin through Heavy with italics throughout.

name: Rules
classification_notes: The page content and style listing suggest a neutral, upright grotesque sans-serif with a wide width range spanning Compressed through Expanded, typical of contemporary workhorse grotesque families.
summary: A large variable grotesque from Blaze Type spanning five widths and nine weights, with italics throughout all 90 styles.
description: Covering Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded widths, the family offers nine weights from Thin to Black each with a matching italic across all five width variants. The variable font format ties the full range into a single file, making it practical for responsive and multi-platform work. A broad, functional grotesque built for demanding layout systems rather than a single editorial voice.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Rules as a 90-style variable font collection organised into five families — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded — each running from Thin to Black with italics throughout.

name: Solae
classification_notes: A revival inspired by Romain du Roi, the late 17th-century transitional serif commissioned by Louis XIV, placing it firmly in the transitional category with its high contrast and vertical stress, though given a distinctive contemporary treatment with a rotalic variant instead of a conventional italic.
summary: A Romain du Roi revival by Gwennina Moigne that replaces the conventional italic with a 9° rotated "rotalic" cut, accompanied by over 2,000 glyphs including astrological symbols and extensive ligature sets.
description: Designed by Gwennina Moigne and released in 2025, Solae takes the Romain du Roi as its historical anchor whilst introducing a rotalic style, a 9° rotation meant to echo orbital movement, in place of a traditional sloped roman. The family carries over 2,082 glyphs covering 100+ languages, moon phase symbols, astrological characters and a large array of uppercase and lowercase ligatures suited to editorial, poster and identity work. The celestial framing and the Sun King namecheck are conceptually coherent rather than decorative: the delicate stroke contrast and rigorous structure stay close to the Romain du Roi's origins.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Solae as a font shaped by the movement of planets, stars and cosmic trajectories, inspired by Romain du Roi, the typeface commissioned by Louis XIV, the Sun King (Roi Soleil), from whom the name is drawn. The Light style is accompanied by a Rotalic version, a 9° rotated design echoing orbital movement. With over 2,082 glyphs, the family includes astrological symbols, moon phases and extensive ligatures, supporting 100+ languages for editorial layouts, titles, posters and visual identities.

name: Massilia
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans-serif structured across five width families from Condensed to Wide, with oblique rather than true italic styles, consistent with the grotesque tradition.
summary: A variable grotesque spanning five widths from Condensed to Wide, each in seven weights with matching obliques, totalling 70 styles.
description: The family is structured across five distinct width families — Condensed, Narrow, Normal, Extended and Wide — each offering seven weights from Extralight to Black with corresponding obliques. All five families are available as variable fonts, giving designers continuous control over both weight and width axes. The breadth of the system makes it suited to anything from tight editorial columns to large-scale display work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Massilia as a collection of five families across a width spectrum from Condensed to Wide, with 70 styles in total delivered as variable fonts.

name: Big Druto
classification_notes: Low contrast, squarish letterforms with a very large x-height and minimal descenders designed for dense, large-size setting. The reduced humanist influence and tight spacing place it in the geometric sans tradition, though its display-focused construction gives it a distinctive functional character.
summary: A high-density display sans built to eliminate excess white space in large titles, with a towering x-height and squarish letterforms that read as solid blocks of text.
description: Conceived as an extension of Blaze Type's Druto, it pushes density further by maximising x-height, attaching diacritics and stripping descenders to near nothing. The Display subfamily is designed with negative tracking built in, making each glyph a considered piece of a tightly packed whole. Twenty-four styles across two subfamilies cover Regular to Heavy with matching italics, and variable font support is included throughout.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Big Druto as a study in density at large sizes, addressing white space in titles as a problem to be solved. It has a larger x-height than its sibling Druto, squarish shapes with less humanistic swing, and attached diacritics, all designed to appear strong and full at display sizes. The Display subfamily takes this further still, built for negative tracking so that barely any white space remains between characters.

name: Systm Hybrid Reverse
classification_notes: Built from a quadrant and a square on a strict grid, Systm Hybrid Reverse is a modular geometric display face whose construction principles draw from Brutalist architecture and early interface design rather than traditional type classification.
summary: A modular geometric subfamily of SYSTM, constructed from two elementary shapes on a grid and designed primarily as a visual identity tool.
description: Part of the larger SYSTM family by Simon Helmstetter, this subfamily is built entirely from a quadrant and a square, producing letterforms that reference Brutalist architecture and early interface design. Six weights run from Light to ExtraBold with a single variable axis controlling outline thickness, giving designers control over optical density across a wide range. With over 1000 glyphs and coverage for Latin European and Cyrillic scripts, it is pitched squarely at identity and branding work with a systematic, grid-driven sensibility.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Systm Hybrid Reverse as one subfamily within SYSTM, a modular metric family constructed from two components — a quadrant and a square — displayed on a grid. They cite paving, Brutalist architecture, early interface design and modular design as reference points, and position SYSTM as an efficient visual identity tool with a variable axis controlling outline thickness, over 1000 glyphs, and support for Latin and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Recorder
classification_notes: Recorder is a display-oriented geometric sans with distinctive structural variants including capsule, mixed and outline treatments, plus forward and backslanted cuts that push it firmly into the display category.
summary: A 27-style variable display family from Blaze Type, built across four structural subfamilies with forward and backslanted cuts alongside the upright.
description: Spanning Basic, Capsule, Mixed and Outline subfamilies, Recorder offers six weights from Regular to Black each available in upright, slant and backslant cuts. The variable font format ties the full range together, and the structural variation between subfamilies gives the collection genuine breadth for display and editorial work. The backslanted cuts in particular give it an unusual directional energy that sets it apart from straightforward geometric sans releases.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Recorder as a collection of four families — Basic, Capsule, Mixed and Outline — totalling 27 styles with variable font support, offering slant, upright and backslanted cuts across six weights from Regular to Black.

name: Amsterdam Grotesk
classification_notes: A revival of a wood type grotesque from a Brussels workshop, the page explicitly frames it within the Grotesque/Gothic tradition with calligraphic inflections in its terminal strokes and bowl forms.
summary: A revival of a wood type grotesque sourced from a Brussels printing workshop, drawn with calligraphic terminal strokes and generous bowl forms across nine weights.
description: Designed by Yann Esnault and released in 2025, the family traces its origins to wooden type discovered at Unitype, an old Brussels workshop. The letterforms retain the idiosyncratic charm of their source material while being stabilised for contemporary use in editorial systems, signage and branding. All 18 styles are available as a variable font spanning weight and italic axes.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Amsterdam Grotesk as a generous reinterpretation of the Grotesque/Gothic tradition, distinguished by calligraphic ending strokes, ample bowls and unexpected charm inherited from wood type found in a Brussels printing workshop. Designed by Yann Esnault and released in 2025, it is intended as a workhorse family suited to editorial environments, signage systems and global visual identities.

name: Proximity
classification_notes: A display collection built on a shared sans-serif skeleton across seven structurally distinct subfamilies defined by textural infill treatments — braided strokes, dots, horizontal lines, pixel grids, vertical lines, nibbled edges and pearls — placing it firmly in the decorative/display category.
summary: A 90-style display collection from Blaze Type that applies seven distinct textural treatments — braiding, dotting, pixel grids, horizontal and vertical lines, nibbled edges and pearls — to a shared sans-serif structure.
description: Each of the seven subfamilies shares the same underlying letterform geometry but is differentiated entirely by its infill treatment, from fine horizontal rules and pixel grids to pearl-like strokes and braided paths. Three weights run through each subfamily with matching italics, and the dot, pixel, HLine and VLine families carry additional density variants numbered 25 through 100, giving designers granular control over fill saturation. The system suits editorial, fashion and identity work where typographic texture is part of the visual language.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Proximity as a collection of seven families totalling 90 styles, each sharing a common structure but distinguished by a unique textural treatment applied to the strokes — including braids, dots, horizontal and vertical lines, pixel fills, nibbled edges and pearls — across three weights with italics.

name: Ater
classification_notes: Ater blends classic serif proportions with contemporary display tendencies, featuring high contrast between its finest and darkest weights. The foundry describes it as drawing from classic serif typefaces while embracing more contemporary forms, which aligns with a transitional approach.
summary: A variable serif by Nicolas Dupuis spanning eight weights from Extralight to Black, built for both text and display use with high contrast between its extremes.
description: Designed to work across both body text and large-scale display applications, the family draws on classic serif models while leaning into contemporary forms. The variable file allows letter blackening that pushes the darkest styles towards display territory, creating a deliberate tension between legibility at small sizes and graphic weight at large ones. The name references the Latin word for matte black, gesturing at the family's appetite for filling a white page.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Ater as a family intended for both text editing and display applications, adaptable from pocket books to large posters. They note it borrows from classic serifs and more contemporary forms, with a variable letter blackening approach that creates contrast between the finest and darkest styles. Released in 2025 and designed by Nicolas Dupuis, it supports Latin European languages.

name: Chiswell
classification_notes: The family's optical size split into Large and Normal subfamilies, combined with its range of cap-height variants (Tall, Median, Short), points to a text-oriented transitional serif designed for fine typographic control across body and display contexts.
summary: A 40-style serif family from Blaze Type split across two optical subfamilies, with the Normal cut offering three cap-height variants for fine control over vertical rhythm.
description: The family divides into Chiswell Large, intended for display and headline use, and Chiswell Normal, which runs across Tall, Median and Short cap-height variants to give precise command over text density and line spacing. Both subfamilies span five weights from Light to Black with matching italics, and the full collection is available as a variable font. The cap-height axis in the Normal family is an unusual structural decision that makes Chiswell particularly well suited to complex editorial and publishing contexts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Chiswell as a collection of two families totalling 40 styles with variable font support, comprising a Large family for display use and a Normal family offering three cap-height variants — Tall, Median and Short — across five weights with italics throughout.

name: Apoc Sans
classification_notes: A sharp, expressive sans-serif with high-contrast letterforms and strong editorial character, positioned explicitly for bold display and standout heading use rather than text setting.
summary: A sharp display sans with six weights and variable font support, extending the Apoc family into sans-serif territory with Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage.
description: Part of Blaze Type's Apoc family, this sans branch brings the same edged, expressive character to a design spanning Hairline through Dark across 12 styles. The variable font allows fluid interpolation across the weight axis, and the character set covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek. Cyrillic was contributed by Ilya Ruderman of Tomorrow Type and Yury Ostromentsky of CSTM Fonts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Apoc Sans as embodying the battle between Light and Dark. The Apoc(alypse) family, defined by its sharp character set and alternates, was originally released in 2018 and updated in 2020 and 2022 by Matthieu Salvaggio. The 2025 sans release is credited to Matthieu Salvaggio and Léon Hugues, with Cyrillic designed by Ilya Ruderman and Yury Ostromentsky. Blaze Type position it as the most popular typeface of standout contemporary independent design, suited to bold display and heavy font uses.

name: An Corp
designer: Matthieu Salvaggio, Léon Hugues, Pauline Fourest
classification_notes: The page describes An Corp as a hybrid Gothic typeface with low contrast, orthogonal terminations and slab serifs inherited from its pixel-font source, placing it firmly in the slab category with a blackletter-influenced Gothic character.
summary: A revival of the in-game text font from 1997's Ultima Online, redrawn from low-resolution pixels into a full Latin typeface with Lombardic capitals and slab serif construction.
description: Each glyph was traced over the original pixel letterforms to preserve their proportions, inheriting low contrast, orthogonal terminations and slab serifs that make it readable at small sizes. The collision of Lombardic uppercase characters with more contemporary lowercases produces a deliberately anachronistic texture. Its name draws from the Resurrection spell in Ultima Online, and the project pays tribute to the late Micael Priest, who is believed to have created the original in-game font.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe An Corp as a revival of the text font from the 1997 MMORPG Ultima Online, originally drawn at low resolution. The project sought a balance between fidelity and reinterpretation, preserving the source's proportions and rhythm while extending it into a complete Latin set. Following research and a conversation with former creative director Starr Long, the original font work is attributed to Micael Priest, who passed away in 2018, and An Corp is presented as a homage to his work and to the world of Ultima Online.

name: Khalos
classification_notes: Long spiky serifs with high contrast round shapes place Khalos firmly in the display serif category; it is explicitly designed for headlines and large sizes, not continuous text.
summary: A high-contrast display serif with long spiky serifs, originally conceived from a 2002 Ultima Online roleplaying shard and later expanded into a full variable family.
description: Twelve styles run from Regular to Black with true italics throughout, available as a variable font. The design bridges historical calligraphic ductus with a contemporary approach to width rhythm, producing letterforms suited to posters, book covers, website headers and video game interfaces. Its barbed, high-contrast character makes it a poor choice for body text but a distinctive one at large sizes.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Khalos as a font inspired by the adventures of a group of Darshanides on the video game Ultima Online, specifically the Dark Prophecy shard circa 2002. Its long spiky serifs and highly contrasted round shapes balance elegance with barbed feeling, bridging historical calligraphic ductus with a modern approach to width rhythm. Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and Léon Hugues, it was updated in 2025 with multiple weights and true italics, and is intended for heavy display use in headlines, posters, books, websites, apps and video game screens.

name: Phill Soft
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with softened terminals and rounded details, offered across nine width variants from Compressed to Wide, suggesting a contemporary geometric humanist character without serifs.
summary: A 243-style geometric sans spanning nine widths from Compressed to Wide, each with nine weights and upright, slanted and backslanted cuts, available as a variable font.
description: Phill Soft covers an unusually broad width range, running from Compressed through to Wide across nine discrete families, each offering nine weights from Thin to Black. Each weight comes in three inclinations: upright, Slant and Backslanted, giving designers fine control over emphasis and rhythm. The variable font format makes the full span of width and weight axes accessible without managing hundreds of static files.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Phill Soft as a collection of 243 styles across nine width-based families, available as a variable font. The family runs from Compressed to Wide, with nine weights per width and slanted and backslanted variants throughout.

name: Sature Rounded
classification_notes: Rounded terminals on a geometric sans structure, derived from large-print lettering drawn on paper; the absence of sharp angles and the horizontal expansion give it a distinctly soft geometric character.
summary: A rounded geometric sans by Nicolas Dupuis, derived from large-print paper lettering and built around horizontal expansion for high-impact display use.
description: Drawn initially from large-print lettering on paper, the family replaces all sharp angles with rounded terminals and uses horizontal processing to give each letter a full, felt-tip quality. Five weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, each with an italic, are available as static fonts and as a single variable file. The name references the French word for saturation, and the family is intended for stacked, layered layouts where weight contrasts produce an overlap or doubling effect.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sature Rounded as originating from large-print lettering drawn on paper, created with the ambition of building a complete family that unfolds. With all sharp angles removed, it offers a flexible way to convey good vibes, and its 10 styles plus variable font support layering and saturation effects in layout. The name derives from the French word 'saturer', meaning to overtake with excess.

name: Macrosoma Slab
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly named a slab, with squared serifs consistent across nine width families. The breadth of the width axis from Compressed to Wide, combined with slab serif construction, places it squarely in the slab serif category.
summary: A slab serif family of 81 styles spanning nine width grades from Compressed to Wide, each available in nine weights from Hairline to Extrabold.
description: The family is built around a two-axis variable font covering width and weight, producing a grid of 81 static styles across nine families. Width variants run from Compressed through to Wide, making it suited to applications from tight headline stacking to spacious display setting. The nine weights, from Hairline to Extrabold, give each width grade enough range for both editorial and branding work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Macrosoma Slab as an 81-style collection organised into nine width families, each offering nine weights from Hairline to Extrabold, and available as a variable font.

name: Macrosoma Slab Stencil
classification_notes: A slab serif family with stencil interruptions across all styles, spanning nine width variants from Ultra Condensed to Wide. The stencil treatment applied to squared slab forms places it firmly in the display slab category.
summary: A stencil slab family from Blaze Type spanning nine widths and nine weights, giving 81 styles for display and poster work.
description: The family applies a stencil treatment consistently across a full slab serif structure, running from Ultra Condensed through to Wide in a single sweep of nine width variants. Each width carries the same nine-step weight range from Hairline to Extrabold, making it unusually systematic for a display family. The stencil cuts suit signage, editorial headlines and industrial-flavoured branding where legibility under constraint is part of the aesthetic.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Macrosoma Slab Stencil as a collection of nine families, each covering nine weights from Hairline to Extrabold, totalling 81 styles across widths ranging from Ultra Condensed to Wide.

name: Bloyd
classification_notes: Bloyd presents as a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with no visible serif or slab characteristics, offered across three width variants — Normal, Condensed and Compressed — suggesting a utility-focused grotesque family.
summary: A three-width grotesque from Blaze Type spanning Normal, Condensed and Compressed cuts across eight weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Forty styles cover three distinct width families — Normal, Condensed and Compressed — each running from ExtraLight to Black with full italic companions. Variable font support runs across the entire collection, making it practical for responsive and editorial contexts where width and weight need to shift fluidly. The range of widths gives it flexibility across tight column settings and large display work alike.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Bloyd as a collection of three families — Comp, Cond and Normal — comprising 40 styles with variable font support across the full range.

name: Druto
classification_notes: A text-oriented grotesque with strong horizontal emphasis, high x-height, flat counter-forms and low contrast, engineered specifically for tight line spacing in dense continuous reading.
summary: A text grotesque by Adrien Troy engineered to sustain legibility at minimal line spacing, using a high x-height, flat counter-forms and strong horizontal rhythm across three widths.
description: Released in 2025, Druto addresses the tension between space-saving and reading comfort without simply increasing the font size. Its flat counter-forms, horizontal arches and low stroke contrast reinforce a strong optical baseline, letting it hold up at line spacings that would compromise most text faces. The family spans 24 styles across Compressed, Condensed and Normal widths, each in four weights from Regular to Black with matching italics, and is available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Druto as conceived to resolve the contradiction between space-saving and legibility, drawing on the logic of stackable chairs as a metaphor for minimal vertical clutter. The design targets the lowest possible line spacing in prolonged reading contexts, achieved through very short ascenders and descenders, high arches that open the counters, and flat horizontal forms that keep the typeface wide and comfortable at tight settings.

name: Big Sans
classification_notes: Blaze Type describe it as blending grotesque precision with humanist warmth, rooted in mid-century modernism with geometric structural influences; the dual-voice design shifts between these registers via a stylistic set rather than separate subfamilies.
summary: A nine-weight grotesque by Karol Mularczyk that toggles between functional mid-century forms and warmer humanist proportions via a stylistic set.
description: Designed by Karol Mularczyk and released in 2025, Big Sans pairs grotesque precision with humanist rhythm across nine weights, each accompanied by a slanted style. Stylistic set 7 shifts the terminals from angled to vertically cut and loosens the proportions, effectively giving the family two distinct tonal registers within a single typeface. High-contrast internal cuts add a graphic sharpness that keeps the design current without abandoning its modernist footing.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Big Sans as a contemporary sans with two distinct voices: a default style grounded in grotesque precision and mid-century modernism with subtle geometric influences, and an alternate mode activated via stylistic set 7 that brings vertically cut terminals, more fluid proportions and a humanist feel. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching Slanted style, are offered as static and variable fonts covering Latin European languages.

name: Rules Serif
classification_notes: A serif family spanning five width variants from Compressed to Expanded, with a full weight range and italics throughout, suggesting a contemporary workhorse serif designed for versatile typographic systems.
summary: A 90-style variable serif from Blaze Type spanning five widths and nine weights, each with matching italics.
description: The family runs from Compressed to Expanded across five sub-families, each offering nine weights from Thin to Black with italic counterparts. With 90 styles and a variable font format, it covers a broad range of typographic needs from tight display headlines to extended body settings. The sheer width range makes it suited to publication design and identity systems that require width-based contrast rather than a change of typeface.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Rules Serif as a collection of five families — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded — totalling 90 styles and available as a variable font, with weights running from Thin to Black each with an italic companion.

name: Macrosoma Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of the Macrosoma grotesque family, defined by interrupted strokes that create the characteristic stencil cut. The display category applies given the stencil treatment and the range of widths from Ultra Condensed to Wide, which point toward poster and headline use.
summary: A stencil grotesque spanning nine width variants and nine weights, giving 81 styles across a spectrum from Ultra Condensed to Wide.
description: Built as a stencil counterpart to the Macrosoma grotesque, the family runs from Ultra Condensed to Wide with nine width variants, each carrying nine weights from Hairline to Extrabold. The stencil cuts are consistent across all widths, making the system suited to poster work, packaging and any context where industrial texture is wanted at scale. The absence of italics keeps the focus squarely on width and weight as the primary axes of contrast.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Macrosoma Stencil as a 81-style collection organised into nine width families, from Compressed and Ultra Condensed through to Wide, each available in nine weights spanning Hairline to Extrabold.

name: Quadro
classification_notes: Quadro is a clean geometric sans structured around a systematic width axis, spanning five width families from Compressed to Wide with no serif construction present.
summary: A 60-style variable sans from Blaze Type spanning five width families, from Compressed to Wide, each in six weights with matching slants.
description: Five width families run from Compressed through Narrow, Normal and Moderate to Wide, each offering six weights from Thin to ExtraBold and a slanted counterpart for every cut. The variable font format ties all 60 styles into continuous axes, making it practical for layout work that demands tight column setting and large display use within the same typeface. A workmanlike system with broad coverage but limited personality at any single setting.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Quadro as a collection of five families spanning a full width range, available as variable fonts across 60 styles covering compressed through wide widths.

name: Phill
classification_notes: A broad sans-serif system with no serifs and a grotesque construction, spanning nine width grades from Compressed to Wide with upright, slant and backslant variants throughout.
summary: A 243-style sans-serif system spanning nine width grades from Compressed to Wide, each offered in nine weights with upright, slant and backslant variants.
description: Nine sub-families cover an unusually granular width spectrum, running from Compressed through SemiCondensed and Normal up to Wide, giving designers fine-grained control over measure and texture. Each sub-family carries nine weights from Thin to Black, and every weight is accompanied by both a forward slant and a backslant, yielding 243 styles in total. The variable font consolidates this range into a single file, making it well suited to editorial systems and display work that demands consistent DNA across varied contexts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Phill as a large collection of 243 styles across nine families, ranging from Compressed to Wide, with nine weights per family and slant and backslanted variants throughout, available as a variable font.

name: Booster
classification_notes: Booster is a large geometric sans-serif system spanning multiple stylistic sub-families including inktrap, rounded, stencil, polygonal and text variants, all sharing a common structural skeleton across variable widths and weights.
summary: A 222-style variable type system from Blaze Type, comprising eight stylistically distinct sub-families built on a shared geometric skeleton across three widths and nine weights.
description: The collection runs from a clean geometric sans through inktrap, rounded, soft, polygonal, stencil, pixel and text variants, each available in Narrow, Compact and Normal widths with nine weights from Thin to Black. Booster Sans and Booster Text include italics throughout, while the decorative sub-families operate upright only. The scale and internal consistency of the system make it well suited to brand identities that require both workhorse text and expressive display voices from a single typeface purchase.
foundry_blurb: Booster is a collection of 22 families and 222 styles presented as a variable font system, spanning sub-families including Inktrap, Polygonal, Rounded, Sans, Soft, Stencil, Text and Pixel across Narrow, Compact and Normal widths in weights from Thin to Black.

name: Seraphine
classification_notes: Seraphine is a serif family structured around four optical sizes — Caption, Text, Normal and Display — each tuned for its intended reading environment, suggesting a contemporary text serif with classical proportions adapted across scales.
summary: A 72-style serif family from Blaze Type built across four optical sizes — Caption, Text, Normal and Display — each available in nine weights with matching italics.
description: The family runs from Caption through to Display, with each subfamily optimised for its intended size range rather than relying on a single design stretched across scales. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, give 18 styles per optical size. Variable font support is included across all four families, making it practical for long-form publishing and editorial systems where text and headline settings need to coexist coherently.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Seraphine as a collection of four families — Caption, Text, Normal and Display — totalling 72 styles and available as a variable font.

name: Galice
classification_notes: Blaze Type explicitly describe Galice as a humanist sans serif, citing stroke-width variation, organic curves and a rhythm that echoes handwriting rather than geometric construction.
summary: A humanist sans by Karol Mularczyk in nine weights with matching italics, drawing warmth from subtle stroke-width variation and softened terminals.
description: Released in 2025 by Blaze Type, Galice sits between the crispness of geometric sans serifs and the organic rhythm of handwriting-influenced letterforms. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, are packaged as a variable font covering Latin European languages. Its moderate contrast and rounded stroke endings make it equally comfortable in body text and display settings without requiring separate optical cuts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Galice as a humanistic sans serif that balances modern design with the organic forms of traditional serif fonts. They note its subtle curves and varying stroke widths give it a warm, approachable character, and contrast it with purely geometric sans serifs, which they find can appear mechanical. The family is positioned for editorial design, corporate branding, web interfaces and advertising, wherever a human-centred aesthetic is needed.

name: Dean Gothic
classification_notes: The name 'Gothic' and the broad weight range from Thin to Black across five width variants place this firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition, with no serif or slab elements visible in the specimen text.
summary: A large-scale grotesque family from Blaze Type spanning five widths and ten weights, available as a variable font across 100 styles.
description: Five width families — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Wide — each running from Thin to Black with matching italics, giving 100 static styles in total alongside a variable font. The range from compressed display to wide setting in a single family makes it practical for brand systems that need typographic range without switching typefaces. No designer credit is listed on the page.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Dean Gothic as a 100-style collection organised into five families — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Wide — each offering nine weights from Thin to Black with italics, and available as a variable font.

name: Dean Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif with consistent stroke weight and bracketed serifs across five width variants, from Compressed to Wide, spanning nine weights with italics throughout.
summary: A variable slab serif spanning five widths from Compressed to Wide, with nine weights and matching italics across all 100 styles.
description: Five width families running from Compressed through Condensed, Normal and Extended to Wide give this slab serif an unusually broad palette for layout work. Each family carries nine weights from Thin to Black, all with italics, totalling 100 styles available as variable fonts. The range suits editorial and branding contexts where a single typeface needs to flex across very different column widths and typographic registers.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Dean Slab as a 100-style variable slab serif collection comprising five families: Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Wide, each offering nine weights from Thin to Black with corresponding italics.

name: Eternal Collection
classification_notes: The family presents a clean, upright grotesque structure with no visible serif detail across all widths; the breadth of width variants from Compressed to Wide and the neutral letterform rhythm place it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A large-scale grotesque sans covering eight widths from Compressed to Wide, each with nine weights and matching italics, totalling 160 styles with variable font support.
description: Spanning Compressed through to Wide across eight sub-families, the collection gives designers a single coherent grotesque system suited to anything from tight display setting to open editorial composition. Each width carries nine weights from Thin to Black with accompanying italics, and the whole range is available as a variable font. The Normal sub-family adds additional half-step interpolations between weights, extending the palette further for refined typographic control.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Eternal Collection as a 160-style type system comprising eight width families — Compressed, ExtraCondensed, Condensed, SemiCondensed, Normal, Large, Expanded and Wide — each available in nine weights with italics and packaged as a variable font.

name: Systm
classification_notes: Systm is a modular display collection built around a geometric sans skeleton, with sub-families defined by structural treatments such as stencil cuts, binary fragmentation, dotted pixel fills, origami faceting and floor-level stroke reductions rather than weight variation alone.
summary: A 58-style modular display collection from Blaze Type, built across nine sub-families each applying a distinct structural treatment to a shared geometric skeleton.
description: Nine sub-families share a common geometric armature but depart into radically different surface treatments: stencil cuts, dotted pixel fills, binary fragmentation, origami faceting, hybrid merges and stripped floor forms. Each family runs through six or more weights from UltraLight to Black, and a variable font is included across the collection. The result is a single system flexible enough to produce everything from restrained geometric text to densely textured display work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Systm as a collection of nine families totalling 58 styles with variable font support, each sub-family offering a distinct visual character within a unified system — including stencil, binary, dotted cloud, dotted pixel, cryptic, floor, hybrid and origami variants.

name: Balete
classification_notes: Balete features slender, creeping terminals emerging from twisting stems that evoke fluid calligraphy, but all forms are constructed without curved lines, placing it in a distinctive calligraphic serif subcategory with strong display qualities.
summary: A calligraphic serif by Jad Maza drawn without a single curved line, inspired by the Philippine balete tree and its supernatural folklore.
description: Designed by Jad Maza and released in 2024, Balete takes structural cues from the hemiepiphytic balete tree of Philippine folklore, translating its dangling, strangling roots into slender terminals that creep from angular, twisting stems. The illusion of fluid calligraphy is achieved entirely through straight lines, giving each letterform a taut, uncanny quality that runs from the hypnotic Thin through to a commanding Black. Eighteen styles including full italics make it usable across editorial, branding and display work where a sense of mythology or menace is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Balete as evoked by the eerie structure of the native balete tree, infamous in Philippine folklore for housing supernatural beings. The letterforms feature creeping, slender terminals emerging from twisting stems, achieving the illusion of fluid calligraphy without a single curved line. Roman and Italic styles run from Thin to Black, and the family is developed for Latin European and Vietnamese.

name: Narri
classification_notes: A single-weight display face with inverted contrast and organic calligraphic construction, clearly outside running text use and designed for decorative and branding applications.
summary: A single-weight calligraphic display face with inverted contrast and flame-inspired organic curves, designed for bold branding work.
description: Designed by Elsa Drevous and released in 2025, Narri draws on the dancing shapes of flames to produce a non-conformist calligraphic structure with strong inverted contrast and sharp stroke endings. The design sits outside classical calligraphic convention, blending influences to produce a distinctive character that works as either headline type or ornamental lettering. Suited to branding projects where a strong, immediately recognisable typographic identity is needed.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Narri as a playful display typeface by Elsa Drevous, built from curvy lines, strong inverted contrast and sharp endings that result in organic calligraphic shapes. Inspired by the movement of flames, it deliberately departs from classical calligraphic rules, and its ornamental quality means individual glyphs can function as decorative letters or distinctive standalone shapes, making it well suited to lively branding projects.

name: Sature
classification_notes: Derived from hand-drawn large-print lettering with a distinctive horizontal expansion that gives letters visible fullness; best suited to display use at large sizes rather than continuous text.
summary: A display sans by Nicolas Dupuis derived from hand-drawn large-print lettering, built around horizontal expansion to create a saturating, felt-tip marker quality at large sizes.
description: Designed from paper-drawn lettering, Sature is built on horizontal processing that gives each letterform a dense, expansive fullness particularly legible at display scale. Five weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold each carry a matching italic, and the variable font allows weight transitions that produce a doubling effect between styles. The name comes from the French "saturer", and the family is intended to be used at excess, pushing layouts toward maximum visual density.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sature as originating from large-print lettering drawn on paper, with horizontal processing giving the letters their fullness for use in large sizes and high-impact messages. The variable font creates the impression of a letter doubling between styles, like a felt-tip marker enlarging the form, and the name references the French word for excess, encouraging maximum use.

name: Intermedial Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif family with four named stylistic sub-axes — Solid, Reliable, Classic and Didone — spanning Egyptian-style sturdy slabs through to high-contrast Didone-inflected serifs, all sharing slab construction.
summary: A 56-style variable slab serif offering four distinct stylistic voices — Solid, Reliable, Classic and Didone — across seven weights with matching italics.
description: The family is structured around four named sub-styles that shift the character of the slab serif construction from a sturdy, low-contrast Solid through to a high-contrast Didone, with Reliable and Classic sitting between them. Seven weights from Thin to Black run through all four styles, each paired with an italic, giving 56 static styles in addition to variable font support. The breadth of stylistic variation within a single family makes it adaptable across both text and display work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type offer Intermedial Slab as a single family of 56 styles with variable font support, covering four stylistic variants — Solid, Reliable, Classic and Didone — each available in seven weights with italics throughout.

name: Rondette
classification_notes: A display-weight geometric sans with sharp diagonal cuts and rounded terminals, drawing on Futura, Kabel and Herbert Bayer's Universal alphabet; the athletic, structured geometry and stylistic alternates place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A geometric display sans in four weights, drawing on 1970s Unimark sports branding, Churchward Design 70 and Herbert Bayer's Universal alphabet for an athletic, cut-form aesthetic.
description: Designed by Fabrizio Falcone and Alex Bossi and released in 2024, Rondette combines the circular geometry of Futura and Kabel with sharp diagonal cuts on S, C and G, and rounded strokes on A, E and F. Stylistic alternates include variant legs on R and K, alternate bowl forms on b, d, p and q, and both single- and double-storey g, giving considerable compositional variety. Available as a variable font across four static weights, it suits sports branding, editorial display and identity work where geometric rigour meets expressive detail.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Rondette as historically rooted, citing Joseph Churchward's Churchward Design 70 (No-End version), Unimark International's 1970s work for Agip, Herbert Bayer's Universal alphabet, and 1930s sans-serifs like Futura and Kabel as key inspirations. They frame it as a bridge between those references and the contemporary sports world, highlighting its sharp diagonal cuts, rounded elements and wealth of stylistic alternates as tools for compositions with variety and flair.

name: Indecisive Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface drawing on geometric sans-serif sources including Bernhard Gothic and Spartan, with swash alternates derived from old-style italic serifs, placing it firmly in the geometric mono category.
summary: A geometric monospaced family of 20 styles drawing on Bernhard Gothic, Nobel Antieke and Spartan, with swash alternates lifted from old-style italic serifs like Granjon.
description: Running from Thin to Heavy with slanted counterparts throughout, the family is available as a variable font and covers Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic. Standout characters include the /r, /w and swooping out-strokes on the /K and /R, giving it personality without overwhelming a setting. Heavier weights allow strokes to merge for a bolder, more expressive result, while lighter weights hold up well in longer passages of text.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Indecisive Mono as a geometric mono that merges elements from various sans-serifs including Bernhard Gothic, Nobel Antieke and Spartan, with swash alternates drawn from old-style italic serifs such as Granjon. They characterise it as joyful and distinct, with 20 styles from Thin to Heavy and slanted counterparts, suited to any setting and covering Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic.

name: Indecisive Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans, drawing on sources including Bernhard Gothic, Nobel Antieke and Spartan, with swash alternates from old-style italic serifs adding a playful, hybrid quality.
summary: A geometric sans blending influences from Bernhard Gothic, Nobel and Spartan, with swash alternates drawn from old-style italic serif traditions for added character.
description: Twenty styles run from Thin to Heavy across a variable font, each weight accompanied by a slanted counterpart rather than a true italic. Alternate swash characters derived from punchcutters such as Granjon give the family a distinctly hybrid character, with standout details on the /r, /w, /K and /R. Heavier weights allow strokes to merge for expressive display work, while lighter weights hold up at longer text sizes.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Indecisive Sans as a geometric sans that merges elements from Bernhard Gothic, Nobel Antieke and Spartan, supplemented by swash alternates taken from old-style italic serifs in the tradition of punchcutters like Granjon. The result, they say, is a joyful and distinct typeface suited to everything from newspapers and magazines to websites and packaging, with Latin extended, Greek and Cyrillic coverage across all styles.

name: Aman
classification_notes: Aman is a serif family structured across four optical sizes — Caption, Text, Normal and Display — each with the same weight range, a hallmark of traditional text-oriented serif design intended to perform across size-specific contexts.
summary: A 72-style serif collection from Blaze Type, structured across four optical sizes from Caption to Display, each available in nine weights with italics and as a variable font.
description: The family divides into Caption, Text, Normal and Display subfamilies, each tuned to perform at a specific size range and carrying the full weight spectrum from Thin to Black with matching italics. All four optical variants are also available as variable fonts, giving a combined 72 static styles plus variable flexibility. The structure makes it well suited to complex editorial and publishing projects that need consistent character across body text and headline settings.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Aman as a collection of four families — Caption, Text, Normal and Display — totalling 72 styles with variable font support across all four optical sizes.

name: Collapse
classification_notes: A condensed serif with sharp, high-contrast letterforms and compressed proportions, sitting in the display serif category with strong expressive and editorial qualities.
summary: A condensed display serif with sharp, high-contrast letterforms drawn in four weights, loosely inspired by the title typography of Neon Genesis Evangelion.
description: Designed by Axel Keussel Andre and released in 2024, it runs from Thin to Bold with matching italics and is also available as a variable font. The compressed proportions and angular details give it a taut, severe quality suited to editorial headers, posters and title sequences. Coverage extends across Latin European languages.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Collapse as an austere and melancholic condensed serif crafted with stark pessimism, loosely inspired by the iconic serif from Neon Genesis Evangelion's opening titles. They characterise it as balancing elegance and intensity, with lighter weights offering delicate restraint and heavier weights delivering a confrontational urgency, positioning it for editorial work, posters and titles that demand a bold and evocative presence.

name: Holise
classification_notes: Holise is a condensed serif with distinctive detailing on the serifs, positioning it as a display-oriented typeface. Its mix of sharp, spiced serif details and condensed proportions places it in the display serif category rather than a conventional text serif.
summary: A condensed display serif by Ines Davodeau with sharpened, spiced serif detailing suited to titles and large-scale text.
description: Designed by Ines Davodeau and released in 2024, Holise runs across eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, all available as a variable font. Its condensed proportions and distinctive serif details give it a character that sits somewhere between contemporary and vintage, lending itself to editorial headlines, poster work and identity. The glyph set covers Latin European languages with broad diacritic support.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Holise as a versatile typeface that blends modern and vintage aesthetics, with a condensed form adorned by distinctive, spicy serif details. They position it as an elegant choice for adding flair and distinction to titles and large-scale text, capable of carrying both a contemporary look and a classic feel.

name: Caryotype
classification_notes: A single-weight bold display face with chunky, rounded geometric forms inspired by karyotype chromosome imagery. The playful, experimental character and retro-modern shapes place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight bold display face by Vicente Granger, drawing its chunky geometric forms from the visual character of chromosomes as seen in karyotype imagery.
description: Designed by Vicente Granger and released in 2024, the typeface takes its name and visual cues from karyotypes, translating the dense, rounded forms of chromosome sets into a bold Latin display face. It covers Latin European languages and is offered in one weight, making it best suited to headlines, logotypes and short-form display copy where its retro-modern personality can carry the work. The generous, playful shapes also hold up at body text sizes, though the single weight limits its range.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Caryotype as a chunky, quirky and geometric font inspired by the general appearance of a karyotype, the complete set of chromosomes. They position it as a go-to choice for bold, charismatic work that is experimental and retro with a modern twist, suitable for both display use and body text.

name: Santiag
classification_notes: A contemporary western typeface with reversed stress inspired by 19th-century Italian typefaces, incorporating geometric counter forms and half-circle serifs. Its decorative, cartoonish character and adjustable base height place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A contemporary western display typeface by Clement Cases drawing on reversed-stress Italian letterforms from the 19th century, with geometric counter forms built around a half-circle motif.
description: Designed by Clement Cases and released in 2024, Santiag takes the reversed-stress tradition of 19th-century Italian typefaces and reworks it with geometric counter forms. The half-circle drives the rhythm of the letterforms, while square angles appear both inside and outside the characters across eight stylistic variants. A variable font axis controls the height of the base, allowing the overall feel to shift from bold display to something more cartoonish.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Santiag as a modern take on the western genre, inspired by the reversal of stress found in mythic 19th-century Italian typefaces. The design incorporates geometric shapes into counter forms, with the half-circle central to the typeface's rhythm and character. Eight styles introduce square angles in both counter forms and letter exteriors, and the adjustable base height gives the typography a cartoonish quality.

name: Kyle Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with uniform character widths throughout, featuring sharp triangular serifs that thicken toward slab-like forms at heavier weights, placing it firmly in the monospace serif category.
summary: A monospaced serif with sharp triangular serifs that shift toward slab-like forms at heavier weights, designed by Alexander Rütten and Olivia Wood as a sibling to the proportional Kyle family.
description: Released in 2024, the family runs across five weights from ExtraLight to Bold with matching italics, all in a single variable file. The triangular serifs that define the letterforms grow more assertive as weight increases, giving the family a character that changes noticeably across the range. With over 820 glyphs covering Latin European languages including Eastern and Central European support, it suits coding environments as readily as editorial display work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Kyle Mono as a non-proportional, monoline serif family and sibling to their Kyle typeface. The sharp, triangular serifs gain momentum with weight, becoming almost slab-like at heavier settings. They note its practical uses include coding, flight arrival displays and comparing amino acids, while on an expressive level it offers an edgy, individual design flavour with over 820 glyphs and broad Latin European language support.

name: Rules Gothic
classification_notes: A gothic sans-serif spanning five width families from Compressed to Expanded, sitting firmly in the grotesque tradition with its range of weights and italics across all widths.
summary: A 90-style grotesque family spanning five widths from Compressed to Expanded, each with nine weights and matching italics, available as a variable font.
description: Five sub-families cover the full width spectrum from Compressed through Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded, each carrying nine weights from Thin to Black with paired italics. The 90-style collection is also available as a variable font, giving designers continuous control across weight and width axes. The breadth of the system makes it well suited to branding and editorial work where a single typeface must perform across very different contexts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Rules Gothic as a collection of five families totalling 90 styles, each family covering the same nine weights from Thin to Black with italics throughout, and the whole system available as a variable font.

name: Sizek
classification_notes: Sizek is explicitly described as an experimental display typeface, with letterforms deconstructed through intentional breaks and disruptions drawn from digital glitch aesthetics rather than any conventional type classification.
summary: A deconstructed display typeface from Sandro Bonomo that fragments classical calligraphic letterforms through a deliberately glitched, broken aesthetic.
description: Inspired by digital lag, corrupted files and screen glitches, the design intentionally disrupts letter structures with breaks and divisions to create unconventional counters and shapes. Eight weights run from Thin to ExtraBold, each accompanied by a standard italic and a more extreme Super Italic cut, across 24 styles in total. The variable file supports the full weight range, and coverage extends to Latin European languages.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sizek as an experimental display typeface that reimagines and deconstructs the classical calligraphic structure of letters, drawing on the glitches, lags and damaged or half-loaded files of the digital age. The result is a fractured, harsh and edgy aesthetic in which letters are disrupted by intentional breaks, reassembled into unconventional shapes, and said to evoke a sense of human nervousness and the chaotic unpredictability of contemporary technology.

name: Daimito
classification_notes: Daimito presents as a geometric sans-serif with a clean, structured character set across three width variants. The slant styles rather than true italics, and the systematic width and weight axes are consistent with a geometric sans approach.
summary: A 42-style geometric sans from Blaze Type spanning three widths and seven weights, with slanted companions and variable font support throughout.
description: The family is organised into three subfamilies — Condensed, Normal and Expanded — each running from Thin to Black with slant variants at most weights, giving 42 styles in total. Variable font support means the width and weight axes can be explored fluidly across a single file. The systematic range suits editorial, branding and display work where consistent character across widths is required.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Daimito as a three-family collection — Condensed, Normal and Expanded — comprising 42 styles with variable font support, covering seven weights and slant variants throughout the range.

name: Silvana Text
classification_notes: Described explicitly as drawing on 19th-century transitional typefaces, combining Old Style pen influence with modern-style refinement; modest contrast and wider proportions confirm transitional classification.
