summary: A text-optimised transitional serif by Siri Lee Lindskrog, drawing on 19th-century sources with an alternate glyph set that adds calligraphic ink spurs and energetic italic strokes.
description: Designed by Siri Lee Lindskrog and released in 2024, it pairs modest stroke contrast and wider proportions for comfortable reading at body sizes with a more characterful alternate set featuring fluid ink-like spurs on horizontal strifs and diagonal crossing strokes. The italic weight palette extends the calligraphic quality with deep junctions and steep turns, and stacked ligatures such as www and xxx add a playful note. It operates as the text companion to Silvana Display, which carries sharper contrast for headline use.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Silvana Text as a contemporary serif with soft yet dynamic strokes, striking a balance between pragmatic utility and warm personality for longform typesetting and expressive compositions alike. Its drawing process was informed by research into 19th-century transitional typefaces, blending Old Style pen influence with modern refinement, and the family includes an expansive alternate glyph set, small caps, stacked ligatures and a variable file, covering printed and digital applications from books and editorial to posters and brand identities.

name: Pahin
classification_notes: The three-family structure spanning Text, Normal and Display optical sizes, combined with the warm rhythm visible in the sample text, suggests a humanist sans orientation designed for sustained reading as well as headline use.
summary: A variable sans in three optical families spanning Text, Normal and Display, covering 36 styles from Extralight to Black across all three subfamilies.
description: Structured as three distinct optical families, Pahin separates its Text, Normal and Display cuts to suit different size ranges and typographic contexts. Each subfamily runs through six weights from Extralight to Black with matching italics, giving 36 static styles in total alongside a variable font. The optical differentiation makes it practical for publications or digital products that need a single typeface family to perform across body copy and headline sizes.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Pahin as a collection of three families: Text, Normal and Display, totalling 36 styles and available as a variable font.

name: Spinner Gothic
classification_notes: Described explicitly as drawing from American Gothic fonts of the early 20th century and German Grotesk lettering, with circular engraving influences from Swiss coinage. The mix of American gothic and German grotesk traditions firmly places it in the grotesque sans-serif category.
summary: A grotesque sans drawing on early 20th-century American Gothic lettering and German Grotesk traditions, originally conceived for a type specimen and named after the designer's mother's maiden name.
description: Developed initially for the Blaze Type Specimen No.1, Spinner Gothic began as a revival of the circular engraving on the Swiss 5-franc coin before evolving into a freer fusion of typographic references. Nine weights with matching italics span Thin through Black, all available as a variable font with over 800 glyphs per style and a range of OpenType features. The result sits between American gothic weight and European grotesk restraint, suited to editorial, identity and print work that calls for classical grounding without period pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Spinner Gothic as a bold yet elegant typeface weaving influences from diverse typographic legacies, beginning as a revival of the CHF 5 Swiss coin engraving before shifting to a looser fusion of styles. Drawing from printing craftsmanship and iconic American Gothic fonts of the early 20th century, it combines the blackness of German Grotesk with classical charm. The name pays tribute to the designer's mother's maiden name, Spinner, evoking associations with textile spinning and woven fabric.

name: Kyle
classification_notes: Kyle is described as a monoline serif with typewriter origins and sharp triangular serifs that shift character across weights, placing it firmly in the slab serif tradition despite its transition toward triangular extremities at heavier weights.
summary: A contemporary monoline slab with typewriter origins, whose sharp triangular serifs grow in prominence and personality across seven weights.
description: Designed by Alexander Rütten and Olivia Wood and released in 2024, Kyle begins as a restrained typewriter-inflected slab at lighter weights before its triangular serifs take on increasing visual weight and character at bolder settings. The heavier styles are particularly suited to headline use, where the serifs give the letters a strong, distinctive footing. The family ships as 14 styles plus a variable font, with an extended Latin character set of over 800 glyphs covering Central, Eastern and Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Kyle as a contemporary monoline serif with a typewriter touch, noting that its sharp triangular serifs change character as weight increases, moving from a classic slab-like typewriter form to strong triangular extremities. The heavier weights gain headline quality, and the family supports OpenType features including lining figures, ordinals, superior numbers and fractions, with over 800 glyphs for broad European language coverage.

name: Surt Rounded
classification_notes: A grotesque sans-serif with rounded terminals across all strokes, organised into three width variants — Normal, Expanded and Extended — placing it firmly in the rounded grotesque subcategory.
summary: A rounded grotesque spanning three width families — Normal, Expanded and Extended — across 48 styles with variable font support.
description: Built as a companion to the Surt family, the rounded variant softens the grotesque structure with consistently rounded terminals throughout. Eight weights from Light to Black run across all three width families, each with matching italics, giving a total of 48 styles. The variable font format allows continuous interpolation across weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: Surt Rounded is presented by Blaze Type as a three-family collection — Normal, Expanded and Extended — comprising 48 styles and available as a variable font.

name: Homie
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a humanist sans with geometric influences, drawing from 20th-century French humanist typefaces. The design shifts from legible text proportions at light weights to more geometric, high-contrast forms at display weights.
summary: A humanist sans by Ethan Nakache that blends geometric structure into its letterforms, shifting contrast as weights increase from text to display.
description: Designed by Ethan Nakache and released in 2023, it draws on 20th-century French humanist type while pulling the forms toward geometry. Lighter weights keep contrast low for text legibility; bolder weights lean into higher contrast suited to display work. The family spans 18 styles across nine weights with matching italics, and is also available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Homie as a humanist sans serif exploring the intersection of humanist and geometric design. Lighter styles feature low contrast in the counter shapes for legibility in text, while heavier weights increase contrast for display use in graphic design. As weights grow bolder, the geometric features recede to give shapes the room they need to occupy space.

name: Sagittaire
classification_notes: The presence of three optical families (Display, Normal, Text) with refined contrast across weights points to a transitional serif design built for range across sizes. The hairline-to-bold weight span and optical size structure are characteristic of a high-contrast transitional approach.
summary: A three-family serif collection from Blaze Type spanning Display, Normal and Text optical sizes across eight weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Spanning 48 styles across Display, Normal and Text subfamilies, the collection is built to function across a broad range of sizes and contexts, from large-scale headlines down to running copy. Each family shares eight weights from Hairline to Bold with corresponding italics, and the full collection is available as variable fonts. The optical differentiation between families suggests a design that takes contrast and spacing seriously at each intended size.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Sagittaire as a collection of three families — Display, Normal and Text — comprising 48 styles in total, all available as variable fonts, spanning eight weights with italics across each optical size.

name: Rules Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif family with unmodulated rectangular serifs across five width variants and nine weights, consistent with a geometric or neo-grotesque slab construction.
summary: A large slab serif system spanning five widths and nine weights, available as a variable font across 90 styles.
description: Five width families run from Compressed through to Expanded, each carrying nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, totalling 90 styles. The variable font format makes the full range accessible across a continuous axis, suiting applications from narrow-column editorial to wide-format display work. The consistent slab construction across all widths keeps the system coherent when mixing widths on a single page.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Rules Slab as a collection of five families spanning Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded widths, each available in nine weights with italics and as a variable font, totalling 90 styles.

name: Routine
classification_notes: Described as drawing from French newspaper sans serifs of the late 19th century, with soft curves and ink-influenced features; sits in humanist sans territory given its warmth and organic quality rather than strict geometric or grotesque construction.
summary: A sans serif drawn from the inky, varied styles of late 19th-century French newspaper type, released by Fred Wiltshire in 2023 with 30 styles and a variable file.
description: The design takes cues from the broad range of sans serifs found in French newspaper printing of the 1880s and 1890s, carrying their soft joints and ink-trap character into a contemporary 30-style family spanning five weights with matching italics and width variants. Coverage extends to Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, with stylistic sets and illustrative glyphs included alongside the core character set. The family is built to move between small text and large display use without switching families.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Routine as a friendly and playful sans with soft edges, joints and curves that blends into a page. They position it as an original take on French newspaper sans serifs from the late 19th century, translating their inky, charming features into a contemporary design. Conservative with its weights and widths, the family is intended for everyday use across newspapers, magazines, posters, websites and packaging.

name: Secular
classification_notes: Secular is explicitly modelled on French Ronde Scripts of the 17th to 20th centuries, a calligraphic script tradition with circular, upright letterforms rather than slanted cursive construction.
summary: A three-weight script family by Bouk Ra drawn from the French Ronde tradition, with circular uppercase strokes and stylistic alternates for display work.
description: Designed by Bouk Ra and released in 2023, Secular takes its cues from French Ronde Scripts spanning the 17th to 20th centuries. The uppercase letters are built around circular stroke construction, giving the family a formal, upright quality distinct from slanted cursive scripts. Three weights from Thin to Regular offer tonal range for logotypes, headlines and pull quotes, with stylistic alternates providing additional variation.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Secular as a typeface family drawing inspiration from classic French Ronde Scripts of the 17th to 20th centuries. They highlight the circular construction of the strokes as a defining characteristic, particularly in the uppercase, and position the family as a tool for designers creating elegant logotypes, headlines and quotes. The family ships in three styles with stylistic alternates and supports Latin European languages.

name: Relais
classification_notes: Named 'Relais Display' across all styles, the family is positioned as a display-oriented sans with compressed and condensed width variants, spanning a broad weight range built for headline and editorial use.
summary: A display sans in three width families — Compressed, Condensed and Normal — running across seven weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Forty-two styles spread across Compressed, Condensed and Normal subfamilies, each offering ExtraLight through Black with italics at every weight. The width range makes it suited to high-density headlines, magazine covers and poster work where spatial economy matters. Variable font support allows continuous interpolation across weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Relais as a collection of three families — Comp, Cond and Normal — totalling 42 styles with variable font support, designed for expressive display use across a full weight range from ExtraLight to Black.

name: Neue Tarasque
classification_notes: Inspired by Roger Excoffon and French humanist tradition, Neue Tarasque blends Swiss geometric rigour with calligraphic warmth, placing it firmly in the humanist sans category.
summary: A humanist sans drawing on Roger Excoffon's French typographic tradition, balanced against Swiss geometric discipline, released as a variable font in three weights.
description: Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and Léon Hugues at Blaze Type, Neue Tarasque positions itself between Swiss International Style precision and the calligraphic warmth of French humanist letterforms. The family runs from Light to Bold with matching italics, offered as a variable font, and covers Latin European languages with an extensive OpenType feature set. It sits comfortably across editorial, branding and screen work where legibility and personality need to coexist.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Neue Tarasque as a font family inspired by the blend of Swiss international design and the character of the Provençal town of Tarasque, drawing on the work of French humanist designer Roger Excoffon. They highlight its range of weights and styles, extensive multilingual character set, and OpenType features including ligatures, alternates and stylistic sets, positioning it for use across editorial, branding and web applications.

name: Avril
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a script typeface, loosely inspired by French sign painting with expressive semi-connecting characters and loopy curves that mimic dynamic handwriting.
summary: A semi-connecting script in five weights drawn from mid-century French sign-painting, with loopy curves that suggest a single unbroken line.
description: Designed by Laurene Girbal and released in 2023, Avril takes its cue from Jean Joveneaux's 1950s French sign-painting manual, translating that loose, brush-driven rhythm into a digital script with low-contrast strokes and expressive letterforms. Five weights from Thin to Bold give it range: lighter cuts hold up in short paragraphs while the heavier styles suit headlines and display work. A variable font file ships alongside the static formats.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Avril as a chill but lively script loosely inspired by a French sign-painting book from the fifties. The font features expressive semi-connecting characters and loopy curves that suggest an unbroken line, mimicking dynamic handwriting with low contrast. Originally a single weight, it has grown to five styles and is available as a variable font. The name is a tribute to spring.

name: Digital Sans
classification_notes: Explicitly inspired by Paul Renner's Futura, Digital Sans is built on geometric construction principles with circular forms and minimal stroke contrast, characteristic of the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans in seven weights with variable font support, drawing on Futura's construction while introducing alternate glyph sets for tonal flexibility.
description: Built on circular geometry in the tradition of Futura, Digital Sans offers 14 styles from Thin to Black with matching italics, all available as a variable font. The family distinguishes itself through two alternate glyph sets, one with diagonal cuts and one with vertical cuts and sharp angles, letting designers shift between a neutral and a more angular character. Latin and Greek are covered, making it workable across branding, headlines and body text on screen and in print.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Digital Sans as a timeless geometric sans inspired by Paul Renner's Futura, designed as a bridge between the 1920s and the present day. Rather than a straightforward revival, they say it questions geometric systems to establish its own language, offering alternate glyphs that allow it to switch between a neutral default with diagonal cuts and a sharper version with vertical cuts, giving designers the ability to modulate its voice across a wide range of applications.

name: Fautive
classification_notes: A highly expressive serif with exaggerated personality, based loosely on the work of Louis Perrin, optimised for display and headline use with calligraphic italics and ornamental features.
summary: An expressive display serif by Fred Wiltshire, drawing loosely on Louis Perrin's work and extending it into 18 flamboyant styles with Cyrillic, Greek and extended Latin support.
description: Built around exaggerated details and a playful character, the family runs from Thin to Black with matching italics, where lighter weights amplify decorative movement and heavier ones round off the quirks. Each font ships with an extensive set of ligatures, alternates, modular ornaments, illustrations and, per the foundry, a snake. The regular style holds up for running text, but the design is at its best in headline and display settings on posters, books and editorial layouts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Fautive as a dynamic and lively serif with exaggerated personality and a playful appearance, originally rooted in the work of Louis Perrin. The family spans 18 flamboyant styles, with elegant and intricate details crafted to captivate the reader, and ships with extravagant ligatures, alternates, modular ornaments, illustrations and a snake. Lighter weights accentuate flourish and movement; the italics retain playfulness while adding calligraphic character. Coverage includes extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.

name: Nuances
classification_notes: The page designates it a serif family; the name 'Nuances Serif' and the range of weights from Thin to Black across three width variants suggest a contemporary text and display serif with humanist tendencies.
summary: A three-width serif family from Blaze Type spanning 48 styles across Condensed, Normal and Extended cuts, each with eight weights and matching italics.
description: The family is built around three distinct widths — Condensed, Normal and Extended — each running from Thin to Black with italic companions, giving designers 48 static styles alongside a variable font. That breadth makes it adaptable across text sizes, editorial layouts and display applications where width contrast between headings and body copy is useful. The variable font consolidates the range into a single file for screen and interactive use.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Nuances as a collection of three families — Condensed, Normal and Extended — totalling 48 styles and available as a variable font.

name: Silvana Display
classification_notes: Silvana Display is explicitly positioned between Old Style and Modern, drawing on transitional typefaces from the 19th century with high stroke contrast and delicate pointed serifs. Blaze Type's own description cites the combination of Old Style pen influence with the sharper contrast of Modern-style refinement.
summary: A high-contrast transitional display serif by Siri Lee Lindskrog, distinguished by ink-spur details derived from a printing artefact found in a 19th-century Schelter & Giesecke specimen.
description: Drawing on 19th-century transitional models, Silvana Display pairs pronounced stroke contrast and pointed serifs with a distinct design conceit: a subtle ink accumulation spotted in a Pennsylvania type specimen has been formalised into a recurring spur across letters including G, S, a and n. An alternate character set extends this ink-like detail to horizontal serifs and diagonal crossings, while the italics introduce deep junctions and steep calligraphic turns. A companion family, Silvana Text, offers reduced contrast and wider proportions for sustained reading across any size.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Silvana Display as a contemporary serif with sharp, dynamic strokes, pronounced contrast and delicate pointed serifs. The design draws on research into 19th-century transitional typefaces, blending Old Style pen influence with Modern refinement. A printing imperfection in a Schelter & Giesecke specimen became the basis for a distinctive ink spur detail, applied across key characters and expanded in the alternate glyph set. The family includes small caps, stacked ligatures and a variable file, and is suited to brand identities, editorial work, book covers, posters and screen use.

name: Slussen
classification_notes: A large-family grotesque sans spanning five width variants from Compressed to Expanded, each with a full weight range and italics, consistent with the neo-grotesque tradition of systematic width and weight variation.
summary: An 80-style grotesque sans spanning five widths from Compressed to Expanded, each carrying eight weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Five width families run from Compressed through Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded, each stepping through eight weights from Thin to Black with full italic coverage. The variable font format ties the entire collection together into a single file for flexible use across print and screen. A workhorse grotesque built for systems that need consistent typographic range without switching families.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Slussen as a collection of five families totalling 80 styles, available as a variable font, spanning a full spectrum of widths and weights with italic companions throughout.

name: Slussen Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of the Slussen neo-grotesque family, retaining the angular, flat-ended construction of its parent while applying uniform character widths throughout.
summary: A monospaced companion to Slussen, carrying over the family's angular construction-site aesthetic into a fixed-width format.
description: Designed by Hugo Jourdan and released in 2022, Slussen Mono is built on the same linear, angular outlines and flat stroke endings that define the broader Slussen family. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and a variable file is included. The uniform character width suits code, tabular data and text-based layouts where consistent spacing is required.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Slussen Mono as the monospaced version of Slussen, retaining its architectural and construction-site inspirations while adding consistency and clarity to text layouts. The parent family spans 6 widths and 8 weights across 104 styles, and includes a Stencil version alongside the Mono, making the whole system suitable for everything from small captions to large titles in both print and screen contexts.

name: Sigurd
classification_notes: Sigurd draws from medieval lettering and Norse mythology, producing an elegant serif with strong structural construction, swash characters and alternate forms suited to display sizes rather than continuous text.
summary: A display serif by Matthieu Salvaggio and Léon Hugues inspired by Norse mythology and medieval lettering, offered in 15 styles including semi-italic cuts and a variable file.
description: Drawing on the visual language of swords, feudal armour and medieval letterforms from the Nibelungen Saga, Sigurd is built around a strong structural skeleton with a generous set of alternate characters and swashes. Five weights from Light to Heavy each come with upright, semi-italic and full italic variants, giving designers considerable range within a single family. It suits editorial headlines, branding and display work where a literary or archaic flavour is wanted without committing to full blackletter.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sigurd as a font family inspired by the Norse hero Siegfried and the Nibelungen Saga, gathering its forms from Norse mythology, swords, feudal armour and medieval lettering work. They present it as an elegant family with a strong design construction, numerous alternate characters and swashes, suited to text layouts and display uses across Latin European languages.

name: Slussen Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of a neo-grotesque family, characterised by cutout letterforms and geometric construction. The stencil treatment places it firmly in the display category despite sharing structural DNA with the grotesque Slussen.
summary: A stencil variant of Hugo Jourdan's Slussen neo-grotesque, built around simplified cutout letterforms drawn from the visual language of construction sites.
description: Eight weights from Thin to Black run across a variable font axis, with the stencil cuts giving each weight a distinct character at both small and large sizes. Designed specifically for signage contexts, the geometric letterforms with flat endings and angular outlines retain the structural logic of the wider Slussen system. Part of a larger type system that includes a standard grotesque, a monospace and a family spanning six widths and 104 styles.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Slussen Stencil as the stencil variation of Slussen, created in reference to construction sites and suited to signage design. The cutout letterforms are bold, geometric and simplified. The wider Slussen family covers six widths and eight weights with matching italics, totalling 104 styles, and also includes a monospace version, making it a complete type system for both print and screen use.

name: Inferi
classification_notes: Blaze Type describe Inferi as rooted in Garalde print-style letterforms, with squared edges and rounded roman finishes; this places it firmly in the humanist/old-style serif tradition associated with Garamond and Jenson.
summary: A Garalde-rooted serif by Matthieu Salvaggio, spanning 13 weights with italics and variable font support, aimed at book, editorial and poster work.
description: Built on the tradition of Garalde typefaces, Inferi pairs squared edges with rounded roman finishes, giving it a restrained print quality that reads well on screen. The italic cuts in a more contemporary direction, lending contrast and rhythm to complex layouts. Thirteen weights from Dante to Black, all with matching italics, make it a versatile tool for both body text and display settings.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Inferi as rooted in Garalde print-style letterforms, with squared edges and rounded finishes in the roman and a more modern approach in the italics, drawn with a careful hand-style touch. They position it across book, poster and editorial uses, and highlight its suitability for achieving a printed-type feeling on digital platforms.

name: Emeritus
designer: Tim Vanhille, Léon Hugues, Matthieu Salvaggio
classification_notes: Explicitly drawn from rotunda and Fraktur traditions, combining historical blackletter structure with high-contrast modern detailing and calligraphic terminals. The foundry classifies it as blackletter and describes its rotunda origins directly.
summary: A single-style blackletter drawing on rotunda and Fraktur traditions, with high-contrast strokes and thin organic terminals that give the letterforms a calligraphic rhythm.
description: Designed by Tim Vanhille, Léon Hugues and Matthieu Salvaggio, Emeritus builds from the historical ductus of rotunda blackletter and layers in sharp, high-contrast details characteristic of Blaze Type's house aesthetic. The thin organic terminals introduce a lively, almost dancing quality that sets it apart from more rigid Fraktur revivals. Positioned for posters, film titles and web projects that require a strong gothic presence without sacrificing elegance.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Emeritus as a modern reinterpretation of the rotunda style, begun by establishing the historical structure through original ductus before enriching the forms with high-contrast details. The addition of thin organic terminals connects to calligraphic ornament traditions and gives the letterforms a dancing rhythm. The result mixes rounded rotunda shapes with the sharpness of the BLZT aesthetic, positioned as a classic choice for posters, film and web design requiring a strong gothic approach.

name: Joly
classification_notes: Inspired by 18th-century Dutch type, Joly sits in the transitional serif tradition with classical proportions adapted for contemporary use across multiple optical sizes.
summary: A Dutch-inspired transitional serif by Léon Hugues, structured across three optical sizes for use from continuous text to display settings.
description: Begun as a revival research project drawing on 18th-century Dutch type, Joly developed into a fully contemporary family of 24 styles spanning Text, Normal and Display subfamilies, each with four weights and matching italics. The three optical sizes are calibrated for different reading distances and scales, making the family practical for editorial systems and complex typographic layouts. A variable font file is included alongside static TTF and WOFF formats.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Joly as inspired by 18th-century Dutch type, starting as a historical revival research project before becoming a full contemporary type design. Designed with complex text layouts in mind, its multiple optical sizes — Text, Headline and Display — make it a complete typographic tool that is, in their words, elegant, eloquent and trustworthy for reaching readers.

name: Mack
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans serif; its geometric structure is softened by angled terminals and brush-influenced curves, placing it firmly in the humanist sans category rather than strict geometric.
summary: A humanist sans with geometric bones and angled, brush-influenced terminals, drawn from lettering found on a map of Mackinac Island.
description: Designed by Nathan Metzler and Matthieu Salvaggio and released in 2021, Mack takes the proportional structure of mid-century geometric typefaces but softens it with curves and terminals that echo the angle of a brush stroke. The spark was hand-lettering on a map of Mackinac Island, whose pointed peaks and varied proportions suggested a different direction from strict geometry. Twelve styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, all available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Mack as a humanist sans serif inspired by the natural, brush-made variations of details originally rooted in geometry. The design originated from lettering on a map of Mackinac Island, whose angled terminals, pointed peaks and varied proportions set it apart from the geometric forms it appeared to reference. Rather than extrapolating those few letters into a full font, Mack is an original design whose structure and contrast echo mid-century geometric typefaces while its curves and terminals emulate a brush stroke.

name: Oroban
classification_notes: The three sub-families — Elegans, Hermonthica and Masuria — each present refined serif letterforms with clear axis contrast and bracketed serifs, consistent with a transitional serif classification. The naming conventions and overall elegance suggest a literary or editorial orientation.
summary: A serif collection from Blaze Type comprising three distinct families — Elegans, Hermonthica and Masuria — each sharing six weights with matching italics across 36 styles and a variable font.
description: Three subfamilies under one umbrella, each with its own character while sharing an identical weight range from Regular to Black. All 36 styles are available as static fonts and as a variable font, giving designers flexibility across text and display applications. The family suits editorial, literary and branding contexts where a considered serif with tonal range is needed.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Oroban as a collection of three families — Elegans, Hermonthica and Masuria — totalling 36 styles with variable font support, designed for versatile use across a range of typographic contexts.

name: Osmose
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a neoclassical grotesk with geometric sans-serif features, combining matching H-height and ascenders characteristic of geometric design with grotesque warmth and cursive-inspired punctuation details.
summary: A neoclassical grotesque with geometric underpinnings from Blaze Type, distinguished by wider capitals and cursive-influenced punctuation.
description: Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and Léon Hugues and released in 2021, the family runs five weights from Ultra Light to Bold, each with an italic, and is also available as a variable font. Wide capital forms and consistent side bearings across all styles give it an even, unhurried texture suited to editorial and cultural identity work. Cursive-inspired punctuation breaks the regularity without disrupting the overall geometric logic.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Osmose as a neoclassical grotesk rooted in geometric sans-serif features, noting that the matching of H-height and ascenders reflects its geometric inspiration. Punctuation details were drawn from cursive old scriptures to relieve monotony in long texts, while wider capitals contribute additional flavour to the reading experience. All five styles share the same side bearings, enabling flexibility and optical consistency across text layouts.

name: Calyx
classification_notes: A blocky, modular typeface with botanical petal-inspired forms and a retro 1960s–70s character, explicitly designed for display use and graphic exploration rather than text settings.
summary: A single-weight modular display face by Anne-Dauphine Borione, drawing on botanical petal forms to produce chunky, retro letterforms with a 1960s–70s character.
description: Designed by Anne-Dauphine Borione and released in 2023, Calyx takes inspiration from flower petals to build blocky, modular glyphs that carry a distinct retro warmth. With only one weight, the face relies entirely on the character of its shapes to make an impression, which it does best at headline sizes and in graphic work. Coverage extends across Latin European languages with TTF and WOFF export formats available.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Calyx as a blocky, modular typeface inspired by botanical shapes, specifically flower petals, crafted to evoke a 1960s and 70s feel. They position it as a playful display face suited to headlines and graphic explorations, noting that its uniquely shaped glyphs bring high character to any design despite featuring only a single weight.

name: Obert
classification_notes: Obert is a modular variable typeface built from capsule-shaped units that rotate and tilt, giving it a strongly experimental and display character; it cannot be classified within conventional typographic categories.
summary: A two-axis variable modular typeface by Maksym Kobuzan in which capsule-shaped units rotate up to 180 degrees and letters tilt 45 degrees, collapsing readable text into abstract decoration.
description: Built from identical capsule modules, Obert lets each unit rotate 180 degrees while the overall letterform tilts in 45-degree increments, producing results that range from legible display type to near-abstract pattern. The family divides into three branches: Obert Variable for the core rotation and tilt system, Obert Bright Variable which uses the same axes to simulate varying luminosity, and Obert Exp which offers five static cuts inspired by physical properties such as chip, thorn and screen. Its name derives from the Ukrainian word «Оберт», meaning rotation. Coverage extends to Extended Latin, Greek and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Obert as an experimental modular superfamily of 24 styles built around capsule-shaped units that can rotate 180 degrees and tilt 45 degrees without distorting the module. Obert Basic is a two-axis variable font; Obert Bright uses the same axes to create a brightness or flickering effect; Obert Exp consists of five static styles inspired by properties of everyday objects. The name comes from the Ukrainian word for rotation.

name: Bonto
classification_notes: Bonto is built from a restricted geometric vocabulary of right angles and curves, designed explicitly for large-scale expressive use, which places it firmly in the display category with a geometric formal basis.
summary: A geometric display typeface by Victor Montet built from a deliberately constrained vocabulary of right angles and rounded forms, designed for large-scale and playful typographic composition.
description: Released by Blaze Type in 2023, Bonto reduces letterforms to a minimal formal system where sharp corners meet circular counters, recalling the logic of modular games like Lego or dominoes. Four weights run from Regular to Black, with the heavier cuts pushing the contrast between solid mass and open round counters. Alternative glyphs for 'e', 's' and 'z' descend below the baseline, giving designers a tool for varying reading rhythm without changing weight.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Bonto as a display typeface built from a simple geometric repertoire, directly inspired by childhood games. The intention was to create maximum expression through minimal shapes, combining right angles and roundness in the manner of puzzle or Lego play. Alternative descending glyphs for 'e', 's' and 'z' allow variation in typographic rhythm, and the typeface is designed to be used large and playfully for impactful, user-friendly design.

name: Mega
classification_notes: Described as a neo-grotesque, the family spans ultra-condensed to ultra-extended widths with rectangular to rounded counter shapes, placing it firmly in display grotesque territory inspired by historical wooden poster types.
summary: A nine-style neo-grotesque display family by Matthieu Salvaggio and Malo Haffreingue, ranging from ultra-condensed rectangular counters to ultra-extended rounded forms, with wooden poster type as a key reference.
description: Spanning nine styles from Nano to Tera, the family traces a path from tightly compressed, rectangular counter shapes at one extreme to broad, rounded forms at the other, all within a single variable file. The design draws on the tradition of wooden display types documented by Rob Roy Kelly, reframed as a contemporary display tool for posters, banners and large-format headlines. Available as TTF, WOFF, WOFF2 and variable formats for Latin European languages.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Mega Script as a neo-grotesque family inspired by the relationship between the infinitely large and infinitely small, with shapes referencing historical wooden poster typefaces such as those catalogued by Rob Roy Kelly. Designed for large formats, the family runs across nine weights from Nano (ultra condensed, rectangular counters) to Tera (ultra black extended, rounded counters), and is available as a variable font covering Latin European languages.

name: Armag
classification_notes: Inspired by early 20th century hand drawing with aggressive pointed terminals and organic shapes, Armag sits firmly in display serif territory, unsuited to running text but distinctive at large sizes.
summary: A two-style display serif by Matthieu Salvaggio drawing on early 20th-century hand lettering, with pointed aggressive terminals set against generous organic forms.
description: Armag comes in just two styles, Fury and Fury Italic, built around hand-drawn letterforms from the early 20th century. The combination of sharp, horn-like endings and open organic counters gives the face a faintly biblical or occult quality that lends itself to album art, editorial headers and brand identities with a dark or historical register. A wider family with additional weights, widths and alternates is noted as in development.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Armag as inspired by early 20th-century hand drawing, offering aggressive and evilish-looking endings alongside generous organic shapes. They highlight its numerous alternates and ligatures as tools for experimentation, and note an old-fashioned print style that gives it an almost biblical appearance, represented by the horned letterforms.

name: Arges
classification_notes: Arges presents as a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with oblique rather than true italic variants, spanning an unusually wide weight range from Hairline to Extra Heavy across Normal and Condensed widths.
summary: A grotesque sans-serif from Blaze Type spanning twelve weights with obliques in both Normal and Condensed widths, available as a variable font across 48 styles.
description: The family runs from Hairline to Extra Heavy, giving an unusually deep weight stack suited to editorial systems that need to cover everything from fine display text to heavy headlines. Obliques stand in for italics throughout, keeping the rhythm of the family consistent across both widths. Variable font support means the full weight and width range is accessible in a single file.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Arges as a two-family collection comprising a Normal and a Condensed, each available across the same weight range from Hairline to Extra Heavy with matching obliques, delivered in 48 styles with variable font support.

name: Sichem
classification_notes: A single-weight high-contrast display serif with Art Nouveau and Art Deco influences, explicitly named a display face and unsuited to text sizes; its dramatic contrast and decorative serifs place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight high-contrast display serif by Anne-Dauphine Borione, drawing on Art Nouveau and Art Deco forms with alternate capitals and dramatic stroke contrast.
description: Designed by Anne-Dauphine Borione and released in 2022, it takes its visual cue from the lightness of hot air balloons, expressed through airy letterforms, pronounced stroke contrast and distinctive rounded characters. The Art Nouveau and Art Deco blend gives it a period-inflected character that sits well in luxury branding, editorial headlines and book covers. Alternate capitals allow selective variation for hierarchy within a composition.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sichem as an exercise in capturing the weightlessness and aeriality of hot air balloons in typographic form. They cite its great contrast in rounded letters as reminiscent of eclipses and sun flares, and position it as combining Art Nouveau and Art Deco influences with a contemporary touch, suited to magazine headlines, book covers, branding and digital interfaces.

name: Gueta
classification_notes: Gueta is a display typeface with a backward-slanting (backslant) structure rooted in urban writing and graffiti tag aesthetics, placing it firmly in the display category with a distinct street vernacular subcategory.
summary: A backslant display face by Malo Haffreingue that draws on graffiti tag culture, built around an unusually rich set of 293 ligatures connecting up to five letters at once.
description: Designed by Malo Haffreingue and released in 2023, Gueta takes its visual cues from the spontaneity and flow of tagged writing, rendered as a formal display typeface with a backward slant. The 293-ligature system is its defining technical feature, allowing letter sequences to flow together with a momentum rarely found in a single-weight release. Best suited to posters, headlines and graphic applications where the connected, kinetic letterforms can read at scale.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Gueta as a backslant font that fuses urban writing with contemporary typographic expression, paying homage to the fluidity and singularity of graffiti tags. They highlight its 293 ligatures, which can link up to five letters simultaneously, as the core of a typographic experience suited to posters, graphics and headlines.

name: Discotack
classification_notes: Discotack is explicitly described as a reverse contrast slab serif, where horizontal strokes are thicker than verticals, combined with organic Art Nouveau-influenced curves rather than the geometric rigidity typical of the genre.
summary: A reverse contrast slab serif drawn from Art Nouveau curves and the uppercase forms of Benguiat's Discotheque, designed for headlines and logos.
description: Brian Dove's single-weight display face fuses the flowing organic strokes of Art Nouveau with the reverse contrast structure of 1960s disco lettering, taking Ed Benguiat's Discotheque as its starting point while softening the genre's characteristic hard angles. Set in all caps, the letterforms create a near-flat top and bottom to each line; introduce the lowercase and a wavy, undulating texture emerges across the text block. Coverage extends across Latin European languages with a thorough glyph set including extended diacritics.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Discotack as a mixture of Art Nouveau and desert disco — a reverse contrast slab serif with organic, flowing strokes and a load of personality. Initially inspired by the uppercase forms of Benguiat's Discotheque (1967), it takes an off-kilter approach to a genre typically defined by straight lines and angles. The all-caps setting produces a mostly flat top and bottom to lines of text, while mixing in lowercase introduces a playful wavy texture. Designed by Brian Dove and released in 2023, it is positioned for headlines and logos.

name: Negatif
classification_notes: Designed with explicit gothic inspiration, letters derived from calligraphic research and cut from black paper to produce raw, incisive forms. The foundry describes it as simplifying the gothic letter in a unique black style.
summary: A single-weight gothic display face by Nicolas Dupuis, built from calligraphic research and paper-cut forms to produce dense, interlocking letterblocks.
description: Letters were first drawn with calligraphic reference, then cut from black paper to arrive at raw, sculptural shapes that borrow from gothic tradition without reproducing it literally. The spacing is a defining characteristic: glyphs are set to touch, forming a solid, almost monolithic block of text that reads as texture at large sizes. Best suited to poster and title work where its dark mass and overlay potential can be exploited.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Negatif as an attempt to simplify the gothic letter into a unique black style, using calligraphic research followed by paper-cut methods to achieve raw, incisive forms. The letters' tight spacing was carefully reworked so they touch and form a solid, gothic-looking block. The foundry suggest using it at large scales for textural effects and overlays, inviting users to reveal their darkest side.

name: Tangley
classification_notes: Tangley is a display typeface inspired by Cy Twombly's looping, gestural mark-making; while it uses Latin letterforms, its exaggerated loops and intentionally compressed vertical rhythm place it firmly in the expressive display category rather than a conventional script.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from Cy Twombly's looping graphic marks, built for tight-tracked headlines and poster work.
description: Designed by Sasha Smirnov and released in 2022, Tangley interprets the untitled graphic series of Cy Twombly through a typographic lens, emphasising the characteristic loopiness of letterforms and a compressed vertical rhythm. The x-height is pushed high to minimise the contrast between upper and lowercase, creating a dense, visually uniform texture when set at tight or negative tracking. Suited to headlines, book covers, movie titles and large-format wayfinding where legibility is secondary to graphic energy.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Tangley as a loose interpretation of Cy Twombly's graphic works, with loopy letterforms, minimal spacing and the ability to set at zero or negative leading. They encourage pushing the font to extremes, including negative tracking and multiple strokes, noting it works best at large sizes in posters, movie titles, quotes and book covers.

name: Square
classification_notes: Square is explicitly a monospaced font in which every glyph is constructed within a square grid, with geometric forms and consistent spacing across all weights. Its fixed-pitch, grid-based construction firmly places it in the mono category.
summary: A monospaced geometric typeface by Raphaël Lefeuvre in which every glyph is constructed within an equal square, allowing vertical and horizontal reading and enabling optical layering across its 12 weights.
description: Designed around the conceptual constraint of a perfect square, the letterforms fill and empty from the inside as weights increase from UltraThin to Heavy, while maintaining the same outer contour throughout. Lefeuvre describes the lightest weight as tracing a park's perimeter, with heavier weights filling its surface inward. The fixed-pitch grid structure allows glyphs to be superimposed across weights, making it well suited to layered or animated display work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Square as a mono font in which each sign is built within the constraint of a square, enabling text to run top to bottom or horizontally. The concept draws on the French word carré meaning a small park seen from above: the lightest weight defines the park by its external contour, while the boldest fills the surface. The outline remains consistent across all weights, with the counter-forms growing or shrinking from the inside, opening up possibilities for superposition and layering.

name: Swirl
classification_notes: A single-weight outline display typeface derived from flat brush letterforms, with flared stroke ends and rounded forms; clearly intended for large-scale display use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight outline display face by Đuc X Cao, drawn from the movement of a flat brush and reduced to an airy skeletal form.
description: Designed during study at Type West, the face began as a solid brush lettering study before being stripped back to its outlines, producing letterforms that are tapered at mid-strokes and flared at the ends. The result reads as bold in colour but light in texture, making it well suited to large headlines, book covers and packaging where a touch of calligraphic grace is wanted. Only one style exists, so its use case is fairly narrow, but within that register it performs with personality.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Swirl Display as a graceful outline display typeface, the product of Đuc X Cao's study into the flat brush's expressiveness at Type West. The letterforms feature tapered strokes and flared ends, originally designed in solid form before being reduced to thin, skeletal outlines. They recommend it for big headlines, editorial, packaging, posters, book covers and video titles where elegance is required.

name: Zoo
classification_notes: A high-contrast display typeface with strong asymmetrical construction, combining sharp and round forms inspired by vinyl sleeve lettering. Its dramatic weight contrast and jazz-inflected character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A high-contrast display family by Quentin Coulombier, drawn from the vinyl sleeve lettering of a French jazz band and built around the asymmetric geometry of the letters Z and O.
description: Originally derived from the lettering on records by the French jazz group Zoo, the design uses the Z and O as structural templates for the entire character set. The asymmetrical distribution of forms gives the family a rhythmic, dancing quality suited to editorial and identity work at display sizes. Two width variants, Condensed and Extended, are available as variable fonts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Zoo Display as a strong, high-contrast display typeface inspired by the lettering found on vinyl sleeves of French jazz band Zoo. The letters Z and O defined the construction logic for the full character set, and the asymmetrical distribution of shapes gives layouts a jazzy, animated quality. The family combines sharpness and roundness, delicacy and strength, with a touch of jazz, and is available as a variable font for Latin European languages.

name: Barabbas
classification_notes: A single-weight display face with hand-lettered character referencing mid-century film title design; its expressive, calligraphic stroke quality places it firmly in the display category rather than conventional text serif.
summary: A single-style display face drawn from the hand-lettered title cards of Richard Fleischer's 1961 Italian-American epic film, updated for contemporary use.
description: Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio, this face takes its letterform logic directly from the hand-drawn titles of the 1961 Columbia Pictures epic, translating their calligraphic weight distribution and curve construction into a contemporary display typeface. Available in one style, it suits movie posters, book covers and large-scale headlines where a mid-century cinematic register is wanted. Language support covers Latin European languages, exported in TTF, WOFF and WOFF2.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Barabbas as a font inspired by the hand-designed title cards of the 1961 Richard Fleischer film of the same name, a religious epic starring Anthony Quinn and produced by Dino De Laurentiis. Designed by Matthieu Salvaggio and released in 2022, it takes a contemporary approach to those referential letterforms, using a design system built around logical curve construction, weight proportion and distribution, making it suited to movie posters, book covers and web headlines.

name: Dudler
classification_notes: A single-weight upright italic with an 8° angle and contrasting narrow-wide letter pairs; conceived for headlines and display work, making it closer to display sans than a conventional grotesque or humanist sans.
summary: A single-weight display sans with a fixed 8° italic angle and alternating narrow and wide letterforms that create an expressive typographic rhythm.
description: Designed by Franziska Weitgruber and released in 2022, Dudler grew from a chance observation of a Swiss truck's lettering in Val Venosta valley. The typeface pairs narrow italic-style characters with wide round ones, and includes stylistic narrow alternates for every wide glyph, letting the user modulate rhythmic contrast at the word level. The 8° slant injects a consistent dynamism suited to posters, web headers and motion work.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Dudler as a design initiated by a petrol station encounter with a Swiss truck in Val Venosta valley. Narrow italic-like letters set against wide round shapes make rhythm the central design concern, with stylistic narrow alternates allowing composers to control that rhythm glyph by glyph. The 8° italic angle adds dynamism, and the face is positioned for display and headline use across posters, websites and motion projects.

name: Taklobo
classification_notes: A heavy unicase display serif with a single style, designed with organic, bivalve-inspired curves and dense letterforms intended solely for large-scale headline use.
summary: A single-style heavy unicase display serif by Jad Maza, whose letterforms draw from the organic curves of Philippine giant clams.
description: Designed in 2022, this typeface takes its proportions and silhouettes from the giant clam, a protected mollusc native to Philippine waters. Ascenders are rejected in favour of a uniform cap-line-aligned unicase construction, producing a dense, majestic texture suited to splashy headlines and bold identity work. Available in a single Display style covering Latin European languages.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Taklobo as a heavy display serif whose proportions and curves echo the giant clam — locally called taklobo — found off the coast of the Philippines. The design rejects ascenders and aligns all letterforms to the cap line, creating a unicase construction that is both rigid and fluid, intended for eclectic visual identities, headlines and unapologetic, statement-making work.

name: Sapfir
classification_notes: Sapfir blends blackletter rhythmic patterning with the minimal shapes of Antique Olive, producing a chiselled display face that sits between those two traditions rather than squarely in either.
summary: A chiselled display face by Valerio Monopoli that fuses the rhythmic patterning of blackletter with the minimal forms of Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive, available in three optical-size cuts.
description: Named after the Ukrainian word for sapphire, Sapfir takes its structural logic from gemstone faceting: sliced construction produces converging lines that collapse into ligatures and generate a dense, refractive texture on the page. Three families, Caption, TITL 50 and TITL 100, address small decorative use through to large-scale title setting. The hybridisation of blackletter rhythm with Antique Olive's blunt, confident forms gives it a voice quite distinct from either source.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sapfir as a chiselled chimera: a mix of the minimal yet unmistakable shapes of Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive and the vibrant rhythmical patterns of blackletter. Like the gemstone it references, the sliced construction creates a multifaceted texture through which cutting lines often converge into ligatures. Three optical-size styles, Caption, Headline and Title, make it suitable for both small decorative use and large exposition pieces in print or digital layouts.

name: Darker
classification_notes: A single-weight ultra-heavy slab display typeface with extremely compressed counters, designed explicitly for titling and headline use. The foundry describes it as a 'super heavy slab display font', placing it firmly in the display slab category.
summary: A single-weight ultra-black slab display face designed around the tension between extreme stroke weight and barely-legible counter shapes.
description: Designed by Antonio Delisiis during studies at ECAL, Switzerland, the face pushes counter shapes to near-closing point, creating dense, modular letter clusters suited to headline and poster work. Short ascenders and descenders allow tight line-stacking, reinforcing its character as a compact titling tool. Available in one weight only, it pairs best with lighter, less aggressive text faces.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Darker as a conceptual super heavy slab display font, conceived to find a balance between the heaviest possible glyph and sufficient counter space to remain legible. The letters are drawn with extremely black forms and heavy slabs, then carved with small white interventions to maintain readability in near-darkness conditions. The tight spacing and short extenders create a playful modular system suited to compact, high-impact headlines across any medium.

name: Apoc
classification_notes: Apoc is characterised by sharp, angular letterforms with expressive contrast, designed explicitly for display and headline use. Its pointed terminals and high-contrast construction place it firmly in the display category rather than conventional text serif.
summary: A sharp, high-contrast display family in two subfamilies spanning six weights, with Cyrillic drawn by Ilya Ruderman and Yury Ostromentsky.
description: Released in 2018 by Matthieu Salvaggio and updated in 2020 and 2022, Apoc comprises 24 styles across Normal and Revelations subfamilies, each running from Hairline to Dark with matching italics. Its pointed terminals and pronounced contrast suit exhibition posters, album artwork and editorial headlines where strong typographic presence is needed. Cyrillic coverage was developed with Tomorrow Type and CSTM Fonts, and the family ships as a variable font alongside static TTF and web formats.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Apoc as embodying the battle between Light and Dark, with a sharp character set and alternates first released in 2018. They position it as the most popular typeface of standout contemporary independent design, calling it elegant, sharp and expressive, and suited to website headlines, art exhibition posters and album covers.

name: Area
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans-serif with inktrap variants across eight width subfamilies, suggesting a workhorse display and text system built for variable font use.
summary: A vast grotesque system spanning eight width families, each paired with an inktrap counterpart, across 11 weights with italics and full variable font support.
description: 176 styles cover four widths from Compressed to Extended, each offered in a standard cut and an inktrap variant, running from Hairline to Extrablack with matching italics throughout. The inktrap subfamilies introduce optical compensation at stroke junctions, giving the family a technical dimension useful at both large display and smaller screen sizes. Variable font files unify each subfamily, making the collection practical for responsive typographic work across print and digital contexts.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Area as a collection of eight families totalling 176 styles, combining four width grades with standard and inktrap versions of each, all available as variable fonts.

name: Rules
classification_notes: A large-family sans-serif spanning five widths from Compressed to Expanded, structured in the systematic tradition of grotesque type families with no decorative or humanist deviation apparent from the page content.
summary: A 90-style variable sans-serif spanning five widths from Compressed to Expanded, each with nine weights and matching italics.
description: Structured across five subfamilies — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded — Rules offers a systematic range of 90 styles built around a nine-weight scale from Thin to Black. Variable font technology ties the family together, allowing continuous interpolation across the width and weight axes. The breadth of the collection makes it suited to editorial systems, identity programmes and any context requiring consistent type across varied measures.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type present Rules as a collection of five families — Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded — comprising 90 styles in total, delivered as a variable font.

name: Sigurd
classification_notes: Sigurd draws from medieval lettering and Norse mythology, with a strong structural construction and swash characters that place it firmly in the display serif category rather than text use.
summary: A display serif family by Matthieu Salvaggio and Léon Hugues, drawing on Norse mythology and medieval lettering for its structural character and swash detailing.
description: Designed with reference to sword shapes, feudal armour and medieval manuscript lettering, the family runs across five weights from Light to Heavy, each with upright, semi-italic and italic cuts, totalling 15 styles available as a variable font. Alternate characters and swashes give it flexibility across editorial, poster and branding contexts. The Norse-mythological framing is evident in the sharp, angular construction rather than applied ornament.
foundry_blurb: Blaze Type describe Sigurd as a font family inspired by the Norse hero Siegfried from the Nibelungen Saga, gathering influence from Norse mythology in the form of swords, feudal armours and medieval lettering. They call it an elegant typeface with strong design construction, tons of alternate characters and various swashes, allowing a wide variety of uses in text layouts and display work.
